Software Lesson 1 Importing & Summarizing Data in R 1.1 Goal of Software Lesson 1 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to import data from a CSV file, examine the contents of the data, and create summary statistics, tables, and graphs for a variable. 1.2 Dataset The data set that will be used for the software lessons comes from the Lung Health Study (LHS). The LHS was a multicenter randomized clinical trial investigating whether smoking intervention and use of an inhaled bronchodilator (Atrovent) would decrease the rate of decline in lung function over the 5-year follow-up period. A total of 5,887 participants (smokers, aged 35-67 years old) were recruited from 10 clinical centers in the US and Canada between the years of 1986 to 1988. They were randomized into three groups: usual care (UC): advised to stop smoking and followed; special intervention with Atrovent (SIA): enrolled in an intensive smoking cessation program & was prescribed an inhaled bronchodilator (Atrovent); and, special intervention with Placebo (SIP): enrolled in an intensive smoking cessation program & was prescribed a placebo. Baseline measurements and measurements at each of 5 annual visits were collected on each of the participants. The data set and a description of the 80 variables can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. 1.3 Importing data Two ways of importing the data will be presented. The first option uses the read.csv() function and can be carried out in either R or R Studio. The second way uses a built-in feature of R Studio, so it can only be carried out in R Studio and not R. Option #1 To import a CSV data set from your computer into R, use the read.csv() function: lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) Let’s break apart the important pieces in the code above: object (e.g., lhs), assignment operator (<-), and function (e.g., read.csv()). The first important part in the above code is saving the data set as a name (or object) in R. R uses object-oriented programming, which means that data are assigned to an object and you, the programmer, are in charge of naming that object. You create objects in R to store information for later use. The name or object created in the example above is called lhs. A good programmer will name the objects in a meaningful way in order for easy recall of what is stored in that object. The next important part is using the assignment operator (<-) to assign information to the object. The other important part in the above code is using the appropriate function to import the data into R. A function in R allows you to specify some inputs in order to obtain particular outputs. The function that is used to import a CSV file into R is the read.csv() function. The inputs that are specified by you are called arguments and are separated by commas in the function’s parentheses. There are two necessary arguments needed in the read.csv() function: file = and header =. The first argument, file =, tells R where the CSV file is located, on your computer or on the web. If the file is on your computer, the easiest way to find the file is through the function file.choose(). This function allows you to browse your computer for the location of the CSV file. Once found, select the CSV file (lhs.csv, in this situation) and click Open. The other argument, header =, tells R whether or not the first row of the CSV file contains the variable names. If it does, then you would type header = TRUE (as seen in the code above). If the first row does not contain the variable names and the file starts with data values in the first row, then the argument would change to header = FALSE. R TIP R is case sensitive, which means that the symbols A and a would not refer to the same thing. Make sure you pay attention to the details of the naming of the functions, arguments of the functions, or objects. Option #2 (R Studio only): Go to File -> Import Dataset -> From Text (base)… Browse your computer for the location of the CSV file (lhs.csv, in this situation), select the file, and click Open. In the new window, give your data set a name (e.g., lhs), make sure the option for Header is selected Yes, and click Import. IMPORTING DATA Purpose: To read in an external data set into R. For additional R resources on importing data into R, see: Importing Data [Quick-R]. Importing Data into R [STHDA]. Importing data with RStudio [RStudio Support]. 1.4 Examine the data 1.4.1 View the dataset Once the data has been imported into R, it’s good coding practice to view your data set to make sure everything was read in properly. One way to view your data set is by viewing the first few rows of the data set. To view the first few rows of the data, use the head() function, specifying the object or name of the data set: head(lhs) ### To modify the number of rows presented, use head(lhs, n=10), ### where the argument n= denotes the number of rows to be displayed The function head() displays the first six rows of the data set. So if you were to read the line of code above, it would read as “I would like the first 6 rows (i.e., head()) of the lhs data set”. The “of” is denoted by the parentheses around lhs. R TIP You may want to add comments within your code so that you can recall at a later time what was executed. To do this, type on a new line # {this is my comment} and R will ignore everything after the #. If you wanted to look at the entire data set at once in the console, you could do so by typing the name of the object (e.g., lhs). However, this is unwise for large data sets, like the LHS one. Rather, it is better to look at the data in small chunks as presented above. There is one exception to the note above. If you are using R Studio, you can view the data in the source window as a spreadsheet. In R Studio, to view the data in the source window, use the View() function, specifying the object or name of the data set: View(lhs) VIEWING THE DATASET For additional R resources on viewing the data set in R, see: head, tail [R Function of the Day]. Get first n rows and last n rows with head and tail function in R [Data Science Made Simple]. Note: Scroll about half way down the page to see the article on the head and tail function. 1.4.2 View data types of the variables Another good coding practice is to examine the data types of the variables in the data set to make sure they are correctly denoted in R. R has 6 data types, but only 4 will be used in this class. A variable can classified in R as a character (or categorical variable), labeled as chr: “Male”, “High Cholesterol”; numeric (or continuous variable, if contains decimals), labeled as num: 15.5, 20; integer (or whole number or discrete variable), labeled as int: 0, 1, 2, 3; and logical, labeled as logi: TRUE, FALSE To view the data types for all of the variables in the data set, use the str() function, specifying the object or name of the data set: str(lhs) R Tip Variables that are categorical but are recorded as 0/1 (or 1/2) in the data set will be treated as an integer data type (i.e., int), even though they are not actually a numerical variable. In some instances, you may have to modify the data type of a variable in R to carry out particular analyses. VIEWING DATA TYPES For additional R resources on viewing data types of the variables in R, see: Data Types and Structures [Programming with R]. Basic Data Types in R [R-Bloggers]. 1.4.3 View names of the variables The last useful tip when examining the data is to view the names of the variables in the data set. This is useful because you want to make sure you are using the correct variable name as R sees it. To view the names of all of the variable in the data set, use the names() function, specifying the object or name of the data set: names(lhs) 1.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 1.5.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the age of the participant in the third row of the data set? 1.5.0.0.2 Question 2 What data type did R put for the race variable, race? For the clinical center code variable, ACLINIC? For the BMI variable, bmi? 1.5.0.0.3 Question 3 Using the LHS data documentation and the output from the str() function, do the data types of the variables in R match the actual type for the variables? If they do not match for a variable, think about why R incorrectly defined a data type for the variable. That is, what is it about the way the data was recorded that made R misclassify the data type? See 14.8 for answers 1.6 Summarizing the data Now that you have the basics of your software (e.g., import a CSV file, examine the data), the next step is to get a sense of the variables’ values in the data set by summarizing them (e.g., counts/number and/or percents; measures of center and spread; graphical displays). 1.6.1 Summarize all variables at once The fastest way to get a sense of the variables is to summarize them all at once. To summarize all of the variables in the data set at once, use the summary() function, specifying the object or name of the data set: summary(lhs) The summary() function provides different summaries, depending on the data type of the variable. For continuous or integer variables, six summary measures/statistics are computed: minimum (Min.), Q1 (1st Qu.), median (Median), mean (Mean), Q3 (3rd Qu.), and maximum (Max.). Unfortunetaly, for categorical variables (that are chr types), R doesn’t tell you any summary information at all about the variable, apart from the length (or number of rows) and the class type (which is character). If the categorical variable is a Factor (another data type in R for categorical variables), then the summary measure/statistic is the count for each category in the variable. The summary() function also provides a count of missing values for each variable, as indicated by NA's :. This is only displayed for a variable if there are missing values for that variable. R TIP Remember to examine the description of the data (aka data dictionary) to make sure you are interpreting the summaries correctly. Recall that some of the variables may really be categorical but were recorded as integers (e.g., 0’s and 1’s; 1’s and 2’s; 1’s, 2’s, 3’s, 4’s, and 5’s). As a result, R will, by default, summarize the variable as if it was a continuous or integer variable, which would not be correct for the variable. 1.7 GUIDED QUESTIONS 1.7.0.0.1 Question 4 What is the five-number summary of number of cigarettes per day at baseline (see f10cigs variable)? What is the mean number of cigarettes per day at baseline? 1.7.0.0.2 Question 5 Which of the following variables have missing values: number of years of education at baseline (yeareduc), weight change (kg) from baseline to 2nd annual visit (wgtchg02), salivary cotinine at 4th annual visit (COT4)? 1.7.0.0.3 Question 6 Does it make sense to interpret the summaries computed for the race variable? Why or why not? See 14.8 for answers 1.7.1 Summarize single categorical variable It is good practice to examine the data when you first import it into your software, and summarizing all the variables at once is part of that. However, it’s most often of interest to focus on a single variable or subsets of the variables. Let’s now shift to learning how to summarize single variables in the data set, starting with summarizing a single categorical variable. The LHS variable that will be the focus in this section is the marital status variable, marital0. To summarize categorical variables, one is often interested in the counts/frequencies and proportions within the categories. This can be displayed in a table or in a graph (i.e., bar plot). 1.7.1.1 Table of counts To create a table of counts for a categorical variable, use the table() function, specifying the object or name of the data set, followed by a $, and then the variable name of interest (e.g., lhs$marital0): table(lhs$marital0) This function produces a count for each unique category in the variable. R TIP Whenever you want to look at a particular variable in the data set (e.g., marital0), the structure of the R code will be object$variable_name (e.g., lhs$marital0). 1.7.1.2 Table of proportions If you are interested in creating a table of proportions instead of counts, you first need to save the count information from the table() function into an object (e.g., counts) and then use that object in the function for calculating the proportions for each unique category (prop.table()). Step 1: To save the count information from the table() function into an object, first, name the object something meaningful (e.g., counts), and then use the assignment operator (<-) to assign the table of counts (e.g., table(lhs$marital0)) to the object: ###Save the count information from the table() function counts<-table(lhs$marital0) Step 2: To create a table of proportions for a categorical variable, use the prop.table() function and specify the saved count object (e.g., counts): ###Create a table of proportions using the counts object prop.table(counts) This function produces a proportion for each unique category in the variable. 1.7.1.3 Bar plot When creating a bar plot for a categorical variable, the instructions are similar to that for creating a table of proportions: you first need to save the count information from the table() function into an object (e.g., counts) and then use that object in the function for bar plots (barplot()). Because you created the counts object previously, that step will not be shown again and will go straight into using the object in the bar plot function. To create a bar plot, first, use the barplot() function, specifying the saved count object (e.g., counts) and other arguments if you want to customize the bar plot (e.g., main= for the plot title and names.arg= for the bar labels): ###Create a barplot using the counts object barplot(counts, main="Barplot of LHS Marital Status", names.arg=c("Never","M","W","S","D")) R TIP If you want the formal R documentation for a function, type ? before the function name (e.g., ?barplot). This is useful when you want to see what other arguments can be called in the function. SUMMARIZE SINGLE CATEGORICAL VARIABLE Purpose: To summarize a single categorical variable using summary measures and graphical representations. For additional R resources on summarizing categorical variables, see: Frequency Tables [STHDA]. R Function of the Day: table [R-bloggers]. Bar Plots - R Base Graphs [STHDA]. Bar Plots [Quick-R]. 1.8 GUIDED QUESTIONS 1.8.0.0.1 Question 7 For the marital status variable (marital0), what does the category of 1 represent? How many 1’s are there in the data set? 1.8.0.0.2 Question 8 Using the bar plot, write down the marital status groups in descending order (from largest to smallest). See 14.8 for answers 1.8.1 Summarize single continuous variable Now let’s learn how to summarize a single continuous variable. The LHS variable that will be the focus in this section is the variable for age (in years) at baseline, age. Recall that common descriptive methods for summarizing continuous variables include measures of center, measures of spread, percentiles, and graphical displays (e.g., boxplot, histogram). 1.8.1.1 Summary measures There are two ways to calculate summary measures for a continuous variable. The first option is to compute the summary statistics for a variable one-by-one, or one-line-at-a-time. The second option allows you to compute multiple summarize statistics at once. Option #1 To create a summary statistics for a continuous variable, use the following functions, specifying the object or name of the data set, followed by a $, and then the variable name of interest (e.g., lhs$age): ###Measures of center functions: mean() and median() mean(lhs$age) median(lhs$age) ###Measures of spread functions: sd(), var() for variance, IQR(), and ###max()-min() for range sd(lhs$age) var(lhs$age) IQR(lhs$age) max(lhs$age) - min(lhs$age) ###Other measures: min() for minimum and max() for maximum min(lhs$age) max(lhs$age) Some of the function names give away what they calculate (e.g., mean() calculates the mean), but for completeness, a brief description for each function will be presented. mean() calculates the mean of the variable, median() calculates the median of the variable, sd() calculates the standard deviation of the variable, var() calculates the variance of the variable, IQR() calculates the interquartile range of the variable, min() reports the minimum value of the variable, and max() finds the maximum value of the variable. R TIP If a variable has any missing values, then the functions above will return \(NA\) for that variable. In those situations, you will need to include a na.rm=TRUE argument in the function (e.g., mean(object$variable,na.rm=TRUE)). For example, the LHS variable for salivary cotinine at 4th annual visit ( COT4 ) has missing values. To compute the mean for this variable, you would type the following code: mean(lhs$COT4,na.rm=TRUE). Option #2 If you wanted to compute multiple summary statistics for a continuous variable in one function, you can use either the summary() function again, but only on the specific variable, or the describe() function. To compute multiple summary statistics at once for a continuous variable, use the summary() function, specifying the object or name of the data set, followed by a $, and then the variable name of interest (e.g., lhs$age): summary(lhs$age) OR The describe() function is located within a package in R called the {psych} package. Because R is an open-sourced software, there are packages being added to R all the time. Packages contain functions, data, and documentation and allows the material to be easily shared with others. You only need to install a particular package once on your computer using the install.packages() function. Then, to use the functions and data in a package, you must run the library() function, specifying the package name, with every new R session. That is, if you exit R and then come back to it, you must first run library(<package name>) to use the material within that package. To compute multiple summary statistics at once for a continuous variable, use the describe() function, from the {psych} package, specifying the object or name of the data set, followed by a $, and then the variable name of interest (e.g., lhs$age). Two additional useful arguments to the describe() function include IQR=TRUE to calculate the IQR of the variable and quant=c(.25,.75) to calculate the first and third quartile (also known as Q1 or 25th percentile and Q3 or 75th percentile, respectively): ############# # Run the install.packages() function once per package ############# install.packages("psych") ###Access the package using the library() function library(psych) ###Calculate multiple summary statistics using the describe() function describe(lhs$age, IQR=TRUE, quant=c(.25,.75)) The summary measures of most interest from the describe() function include the count or number of valid cases (n), the mean (mean), the standard deviation (sd), the median (median), the minimum (min), the maximum (max), the range (range), the standard error (se) (to be introduced in a later week), the IQR (IQR), and first and third quartile (Q0.25 and Q0.75, respectively). 1.8.1.2 Boxplots and histograms To create a boxplot of a continuous variable, use the boxplot() function, specifying the object or name of the data set, followed by a $ and the variable name of interest (e.g., lhs$age), and other arguments if you wish to customize the bar plot (e.g., main= for the plot title): boxplot(lhs$age, main="Boxplot of LHS Ages") To create a histogram of a continuous variable, use the hist() function, specifying the object or name of the data set, followed by a $ and the variable name of interest (e.g., lhs$age), and other arguments to customize the bar plot (e.g., main= for the plot title, xlab= for the x-axis label, breaks= for modifying the binning of the histogram): hist(lhs$age, main="Histogram of LHS Ages", xlab="Ages", breaks = 10) SUMMARIZE SINGLE CONTINUOUS VARIABLE Purpose: To summarize a single continuous variable using summary measures and graphical representations. For additional R resources on summarizing continuous variables, see: Descriptive Statistics and Graphics [STHDA]. Box Plots - R Base Graphs [STHDA]. Histograms and Density Plots [Quick-R]. Histogram and Density Plots - R Base Graphs [STHDA]. 1.9 GUIDED QUESTIONS 1.9.0.0.1 Question 9 What is the mean age of the participants in the LHS study? What is the median age? The standard deviation of age? The minimum and maximum age? 1.9.0.0.2 Question 10 Using the histogram, approximately how many participants were between 30 and 35 years old at the beginning of the LHS study? See 14.8 for answers 1.10 CHALLENGES 1.10.0.0.1 Question 11 Using the counts object, figure out how to create a table of proportions instead of counts for the marital status variable. [Hint: Use prop.table() function.] 1.10.0.0.2 Question 12 Figure out how to make the color of bars in the bar plot for marital status, marital0, to be blue, and how to change the y-axis title to be “Number”. 1.10.0.0.3 Question 13 Figure out how to make the color of the bars in the histogram for age, age, to be orange, and how to modify the binning of the histogram to your liking. See 14.8.0.1 for answers

Software Lesson 2 Normal Binomial in R 2.1 Goal of Software Lesson 2 2.2 Computing probabilities using the Normal distribution 2.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 2.4 Computing probabilities using the Binomial distribution 2.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 2.6 CHALLENGES

Software Lesson 2 Normal Binomial in R 2.1 Goal of Software Lesson 2 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to compute probabilities for the Normal distribution, and compute probabilities for the Binomial distribution. In this software lesson, rather than having data from a sample and computing summaries of the variables in the data set, you are going to pretend that you know the underlying true theoretical distribution of some phenomenon. From this information, you can compute the probability that some event will occur. 2.2 Computing probabilities using the Normal distribution Many standardized tests are calibrated to produce a Normal distribution. One such test is called the RAND-36 test, which is a measure of health-related quality of life. One of the scores produced is a mental health score. It is assumed to be normally distributed with a mean of 50 and a standard deviation of 10. To find probabilities from a Normal distribution, you will use the function pnorm(). This function finds the probability of being less (or greater than) than X in a Normal distribution that has a mean equal to a and a standard deviation equal to b, where X, a, and b need to be specified. 2.2.1 Computing “less than” probabilities in a Normal distribution Suppose you are interested in finding the probability of having at most a RAND-36 mental health score of 33 (i.e., 33 or less). To visualize this: To compute “less than” probabilities using the Normal distribution (value not standardized), use the pnorm() function, specifying the value of interest and the arguments mean= and sd=: pnorm(33, mean = 50, sd = 10) Now suppose that the score of 33 had been standardized. Then its z-score would be -1.7 (i.e., z = \(\frac{33-50}{10}\)). If the value of interest is standardized, then you do not need to specify the mean and standard deviation in the pnorm() function because its default (or preset) values for the mean and standard deviation are 0 and 1, respectively; that is, the default values are set to the Standard Normal distribution values. To visualize this: To compute “less than” probabilities using the Normal distribution AND standardized values, use the pnorm() function, specifying the standardized value of interest: pnorm(-1.7) 2.2.2 Computing “greater than” probabilities in a Normal distribution Now suppose you are interested in finding the probability of having at least a RAND-36 mental health score of 58 (i.e., 58 or more). To visualize this: There are two ways you can compute this value in R: pnorm(..., lower.tail=FALSE) or 1-pnorm(...). Option #1 To compute “greater than” probabilities using the Normal distribution (value not standardized), use the pnorm() function with same arguments as above and also including lower.tail=FALSE: pnorm(58, mean = 50, sd = 10, lower.tail = FALSE) The lower.tail=FALSE argument tells R to not compute the lower tail probability, but rather inverse of that: the upper tail probability. Option #2 To compute “greater than” probabilities using the Normal distribution (value not standardized), subtract the pnorm() function, specifying the value of interest and the arguments mean= and sd=, from 1: 1 - pnorm(58, mean = 50, sd = 10) 2.2.3 Computing “between” probabilities in a Normal distribution Lastly, suppose you are interested in finding the probability of a RAND-36 mental health score being between 33 and 58. Due to how the pnorm() function works, you need to subtract two probabilities from one another (the probability of the upper value minus the probability of the lower value). To visualize this: MINUS EQUALS To compute “between” probabilities using the Normal distribution (value not standardized), subtract the two pnorm() functions, specifying the upper value in the first function and the lower value in the second function: pnorm(58, mean = 50, sd = 10) - pnorm(33, mean = 50, sd = 10) COMPUTING PROBABILITIES USING THE NORMAL DISTRIBUTION Purpose: To compute probabilities of some event occurring when the distribution is known to be normally distributed. For additional R resources on normal distribution functions, see: Using R: Normal Distributions [Champlain College]. {Normal Distribution, Z Scores, and Normal Probabilities in R [MarinStatsLectures]. 2.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 2.3.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the probability of having at most a RAND-36 mental health score of 33? 2.3.0.0.2 Question 2 What is the probability of finding RAND-36 mental health scores between 33 and 58? See 14.9 for answers 2.4 Computing probabilities using the Binomial distribution Suppose the RAND-36 mental health score is converted into a categorical variable-Normal and Frail-and suppose the probability of being Frail is 15.8%. Because the variable is binary and has a set probability for the event of interest, one could consider this distribution to be Binomial and be able to answer probability questions. 2.4.1 Computing “equal to” probabilities in a Binomial distribution If you obtained a random sample of 16 people, what is the probability that 6 of them will be classified as Frail on the RAND-36 mental health assessment? To visualize this: To compute “equal to” probabilities using the Binomial distribution, use the dbinom() function, specifying the value of interest and the arguments size= (for number of observations, n) and prob= (for the probability of the event of interest): dbinom(6, size = 16, prob = 0.158) 2.4.2 Computing “less than or equal to” probabilities in a Binomial distribution Now suppose you want to find the probability that 6 or less of them will be classified as Frail on the RAND-36 mental health assessment. To visualize this: To compute “less than or equal to” probabilities using the Binomial distribution, use the pbinom() function, specifying the value of interest and the arguments size= (for number of observations, n) and prob= (for the probability of the event of interest): pbinom(6, size = 16, prob = 0.158) This function, pbinom(), finds the probability of X \(\le\) value. 2.4.3 Computing “greater than or equal to” probabilities in a Binomial distribution Now suppose you want to find the probability that 6 or more of them will be classified as Frail on the RAND-36 mental health assessment. To visualize this: There are two ways you can compute this value in R: pbinom(value-1, ..., lower.tail=FALSE) or 1-pbinom(value-1, ...). Option #1 To compute “greater than or equal to” probabilities using the Binomial distribution, use the pbinom() function with same arguments as above and also including lower.tail=FALSE: pbinom(5, size = 16, prob = 0.158, lower.tail = FALSE) Notice that the value in the first argument is 5 and not 6. This is because when you specify lower.tail=FALSE, the probability that is found is X > value (e.g., “probability that X is greater than (but not including) 5”). Option #2 To compute “greater than or equal to” probabilities using the Binomial distribution, subtract from 1 the pbinom() function, specifying the value of interest and the arguments size= and prob=: 1 - pbinom(5, size = 16, prob = 0.158) R TIP When dealing with the Binomial distribution in software, it’s hard to keep straight if the value is included or not included in the pbinom() function . To check your answer, it may be useful to add up all of the “exact value” probabilities using dbinom(). For example, in the “6 or more” example above, to verify that you specified the correct probability in the pbinom() function, you can add up all of the individual values that fit that criteria (e.g., 7, 8, 9, …, 15, 16): dbinom(6, size = 16, prob = 0.158) + dbinom(7, size = 16, prob = 0.158) + … + dbinom(15, size = 16, prob = 0.158) + dbinom(16, size = 16, prob = 0.158) 2.4.4 Computing “between” probabilities in a Binomial distribution Lastly, suppose you are interested in finding the probability that between 1 and 4 (inclusive) out of the 16 will be classified as Frail on the RAND-36 mental health assessment. Due to how the pbinom() function works, you need to subtract two probabilities from one another, but figuring out the second value is a little tricky. The code will be provided to you first and then the explanation (with visualizations will follow). To compute “between” (inclusive) using the Binomial distribution, subtract the two pbinom() functions, specifying the larger value in the first function and the smaller value in the second function: pbinom(4, size = 16, prob = 0.158) - pbinom(0, size = 16, prob = 0.158) Now for the piece-by-piece explanation. The first probability, pbinom(4, size = 16, prob = 0.158) is finding the probability of 4 or less (where X is distributed \(Binomial(16, 0.158)\)), which equals \(P(X=4) + P(X=3) + P(X=2) + P(X=1) + P(X=0)\). That would look like this: The second probability, pbinom(0, size = 16, prob = 0.158) is finding the probability of 0 or less (where X is distributed \(Binomial(16, 0.158)\)), which equals \(P(X=0)\). That would look like this: If you subtract the two from one another, you get \([P(X=4) + P(X=3) + P(X=2) + P(X=1) + P(X=0)] - P(X=0) = P(X=4) + P(X=3) + P(X=2) + P(X=1)\). To visualize this: COMPUTING PROBABILITIES USING THE BINOMIAL DISTRIBUTION Purpose: To compute probabilities of some event occurring when the distribution is known to be binomially distributed. For additional R resources on binomial distribution functions, see: R: Binomial Distribution [STATS4STEM]. Note: The useful information is in the first two sections of the page (Sections I and II). Binomial Distribution in R [MarinStatsLectures]. 2.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 2.5.0.0.1 Question 3 What is the probability that 6 of the 16 people will be classified as Frail on the RAND-36 mental health assessment? 2.5.0.0.2 Question 4 What is the probability that 6 or more of the 16 people will be classified as Frail on the RAND-36 mental health assessment? 2.5.0.0.3 Question 5 Why was the value of 0 used in the pbinom() function for finding the probability that between 1 and 4 of the 16 people being classified as Frail? See 14.9 for answers 2.6 CHALLENGES 2.6.0.0.1 Question 6 What is the probability of having at least a RAND-36 mental health score of 45? Software Lesson 3 Confidence Intervals for a Proportion in R 3.1 Goal of Software Lesson 3 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to find \(z^{*}\) values from a Standard Normal distribution for a confidence interval for a proportion, and compute confidence intervals for a proportion. Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) 3.2 Confidence interval for a proportion Confidence intervals (CIs) are a way to make a inference, via interval estimates, about population parameters by using the sample statistics computed from our data. One can compute CIs for any summary measure (e.g., mean, proportion, difference in means, odds ratio, relative risk, slope). In this section, you will learn how to compute a CI for a proportion. There are two ways to do this: manually, by using the CI formula (via the Wald method), or using a built-in function in R (prop.test()). The LHS variable that will be the focus in this section is the variable for those who smoke a pipe at 1 year after starting treatment, f060pipe. The question that you will be trying to answer is: What is an estimate of the population proportion for those who smoke a pipe at 1 year after starting treatment? Option #1: Calculating manually Recall that the formula for computing CIs for a proportion using the Wald method is \(\hat{p}+/-z^{*}\sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}*(1-\hat{p})}{n}}\). When calculating this CI manually in R and from a data set, you need to save each of the following values in objects: the number of those with the event in the variable (e.g., k), the total number of non-missing values in the variable (e.g., n), the sample proportion (e.g., p.hat), the standard error of the proportion (e.g., SE), and the degree of confidence value from the appropriate theoretical distribution (e.g., z.star). Step 1: To count the number of participants who had the event of interest, you first need to name the object that will contain the number who had the event (e.g., k), use the assignment operator (<-), and then use the length() function in combination to the which() function, specifying the variable and the value of the variable that should be counted: k<-length(which(lhs$f060pipe== 1)) #viewing the object you just created k The length() function counts the number of values in the object, and the which() function finds the observations in the variable that match the criteria (e.g., which observations in the f060pipe variable have a value equal to 1, lhs$f060pipe==1). Step 2: To count the total number of non-missing values in the variable, you first need to name the object that will contain the total sample size (e.g., n), use the assignment operator (<-), and then use the length() function in combination to the na.omit() function, specifying the value of the variable that should be counted: n<-length(na.omit(lhs$f060pipe)) n The na.omit() function removes the rows in the variable that are missing, so you would only be left with a variable with non-missing rows. Step 3: To calculate the sample proportion, you first need to name the object that will contain the sample proportion (e.g., p.hat), use the assignment operator (<-), and then use the objects from Steps 1 and 2, dividing k by n: p.hat<-k/n p.hat Step 4: To calculate the standard error, you first need to name the object that will contain the standard error (e.g., SE), use the assignment operator (<-), and then use the SE formula and the objects from Steps 2 and 3: ####Calculating the standard error for p-hat (saved in SE) SE<-sqrt(p.hat*(1-p.hat)/n) SE The sqrt() function takes the square root of a value. Step 5: To find the \(z^{*}\) value from a Standard Normal distribution for a \(95\%\) CI, you first need to name the object that will contain the \(z^{*}\) value (e.g., z.star), use the assignment operator (<-), and then use the qnorm() function, specifying the value of 0.975: z.star<-qnorm(0.975) The percentile value of \(97.5\%\) was used in the above example because you want the middle \(95\%\) of the distribution, so that leaves \(5\%\) in both tails, or \(2.5\%\) in each of the tails. Because the qnorm() function gives us the value where \(X\%\) lies below that value, you are looking for the \(z^{*}\) value such that \(97.5\%\) (i.e., \(95\% + 2.5\%\)) falls below it. R TIP If you want to find \(z^{*}\) values from a Standard Normal distribution for a \(99\%\) or \(90\%\) CI, use a similar code as above, specifying the appropriate values in the function: qnorm(0.995) #\(z^{*}\) for \(99\%\) CI qnorm(0.95) #\(z^{*}\) for \(90\%\) CI Step 6: To calculate the \(95\%\) CI for a proportion, use the CI mathematical formula and the objects from Steps 3, 4, and 5: p.hat + c(-1,1)*z.star*SE Because the c(-1, 1) was added to the equation, the output will give you two numbers: one for the lower value of the CI and one for the upper value of the CI. Option #2: Built-in function An alternative way of computing a CI for a proportion is to use the built-in function in R: prop.test(). To compute a \(95\%\) CI for a proportion using R’s built-in function, use the prop.test() function, specifying the number of those with the event in the variable (e.g., k), the total number of non-missing values in the variable (e.g., n), and the confidence level (e.g., conf.level=0.95): prop.test(k, n, conf.level=0.95) The prop.test() function supplies both a hypothesis test for a proportion as well as the CI for a proportion. If you did not have data and only had summary information for calculating the CI for a proportion, you can just add those numbers to the function rather than saving them to an object. For example, you could have just been given the summary information from the f060pipe variable: 59 LHS participants who smoked a pipe at year 1 after starting treatment and 5597 participants with non-missing values in that variable. Try it out! prop.test(59, 5597, conf.level=0.95) CONFIDENCE INTERVAL FOR A PROPORTION Purpose: To estimate the population proportion using sample data via an interval estimate. For additional R resources on calculating a confidence interval for a proportion, see: Counting Elements in a Data Set [The Analysis Factor]. Interval Estimate of Population Proportion [R Tutorial]. Confidence Interval for a Proportion [BU]. 3.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 3.3.0.0.1 Question 1 What proportion of the LHS participants smoked a pipe at year 1 (after starting treatment)? 3.3.0.0.2 Question 2 What is the \(z^{*}\) value for a \(95\%\) confidence interval for a proportion? 3.3.0.0.3 Question 3 What is the \(95\%\) confidence interval for the population proportion who smoke at year 1 (after starting treatment)? See 14.10 for answers 3.4 CHALLENGES 3.4.0.0.1 Question 4 Compute a \(95\%\) confidence interval for the population proportion for those who the use of nicotine gum at first annual visit after starting treatment (AV1GUM). See 14.10.0.1 for answers

Software Lesson 4 Confidence Intervals for a Mean in R 4.1 Goal of Software Lesson 4 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to find \(t^{*}\) values from a t distribution for a confidence interval for a mean, and compute confidence intervals for a mean. Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) 4.2 Confidence interval for a mean In a previous software lesson, you learned how to compute a confidence interval (CI) for a proportion. You are now going to extend that knowledge to computing a confidence interval for a mean. Similar to the previous software lesson, you will learn two ways to compute a CI for a mean: manually, by using the CI formula, or using a built-in function in R (ci()). The LHS variable that will be the focus in this section is the variable for weight change (kg) from baseline to 5th annual visit, wgtchg05. The question that you will be trying to answer is: What is an estimate of the population mean weight change from baseline to final visit after starting treatment? Option #1: Calculating manually Recall that the formula for computing CIs for a mean is \(\bar{x}+/-t^{*}(\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}})\). When calculating this CI manually and from a data set, you need to save each of the following values in objects: the sample mean of the variable (e.g., xbar), the sample standard deviation of the variable (e.g., sample.sd), the total number of non-missing values in the variable (e.g., n), the standard error of the mean (e.g., SE), and the degree of confidence value from the appropriate theoretical distribution (e.g., t.star). Step 1: To calculate the sample mean of the variable, you first need to name the object that will contain mean value (e.g., xbar), use the assignment operator (<-), and then use the mean() function, specifying the variable of interest: xbar<-mean(lhs$wgtchg05,na.rm=TRUE) #viewing the object you just created xbar Step 2: To calculate the sample standard deviation of the variable, you will carry out a similar process as in Step 1 (e.g., name the object, assignment operator, use the sd() function on the variable of interest): sample.sd<-sd(lhs$wgtchg05,na.rm=TRUE) sample.sd Step 3: To count the total number of non-missing values in the variable, you first need to name the object that will contain the total sample size (e.g., n), use the assignment operator (<-), and then use the length() function in combination to the na.omit() function, specifying the value of the variable that should be counted: n<-length(na.omit(lhs$wgtchg05)) n Step 4: To calculate the standard error, you first need to name the object that will contain the standard error (e.g., SE), use the assignment operator (<-), and then use the SE formula and the objects from Steps 2 and 3: ####Calculating the standard error for p-hat (saved in SE) SE<-sample.sd/sqrt(n) SE Recall that the sqrt() function takes the square root of a value. Step 5: To find the \(t^{*}\) value from a t distribution with \(n-1\) degrees of freedom for a \(95\%\) CI, you first need to name the object that will contain the \(t^{*}\) value (e.g., t.star), use the assignment operator (<-), and then use the qt() function, specifying the value of 0.975 and the appropriate degrees of freedom (e.g., \(n-1\)): t.star<-qt(0.975, df=n-1) t.star R TIP If you want to find \(t^{*}\) values from a t distribution with \(n-1\) degrees of freedom for a \(99\%\) or \(90\%\) CI, use a similar code as above, specifying the appropriate values in the function: qt(0.995, df=value) #\(t^{*}\) for \(99\%\) CI qt(0.95, df=value) #\(t^{*}\) for \(90\%\) CI Step 6: To calculate the \(95\%\) CI for a mean, use the CI mathematical formula and the objects from Steps 1, 4, and 5: xbar + c(-1,1)*t.star*SE Option #2: Built-in function An alternative way of computing a CI for a mean is to use the built-in function in R: ci() from the {epiDisplay} package. To compute a \(95\%\) CI for a mean using R’s built-in function, use the ci() function from the {epiDisplay} package, specifying the variable of interest and the alpha level (e.g., alpha=0.05): ############# # Run the install.packages() function once per package ############# install.packages("epiDisplay") ###Access the package using the library() function library(epiDisplay) ###Calculate a confidence interval using the ci() function ci(lhs$wgtchg05, alpha=0.05) The output for this function provides the sample size for the variable (non-missing values, n), the mean of the variable (mean), the standard deviation of the variable (sd), the standard error of the statistic (se), and then the lower and upper confidence values of the interval (lower95ci and upper95ci, respectively). The alpha= argument specifies the confidence level, where confidence level = \(1-\alpha\). For example, for a \(95\%\) confidence interval, you would specify \(\alpha\) as \(1-0.95=0.05\). The default setting is alpha=0.05 so you do not have to include this argument if you are computing a \(95\%\) CI. CONFIDENCE INTERVAL FOR A MEAN Purpose: To estimate the population mean using sample data via an interval estimate. For additional R resources on calculating a confidence interval for a mean, see: Calculating Confidence Intervals [Cyclismo]. Constructing Confidence Intervals [R for STEPH]. Interval Estimate of Population Mean with Unknown Variance [R Tutorial]. 4.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 4.3.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the average weight change from baseline to 5th annual visit for the LHS participants? What is the value of the SE? 4.3.0.0.2 Question 2 What is the degrees of freedom that was used to find the \(t^{*}\) value in qt(0.975, df=n-1)? 4.3.0.0.3 Question 3 What is the \(95\%\) confidence interval for the population mean weight change from baseline to 5th annual visit? See 14.11 for answers 4.4 CHALLENGES 4.4.0.0.1 Question 4 Compute a \(95\%\) confidence interval for the population mean number of cigarettes smoked at the 5th (and last) annual visit (AV5CIGS). See 14.11.0.1 for answers

Software Lesson 5 Hypothesis Test for a Mean in R 5.1 Goal of Software Lesson 5 5.2 Hypothesis test for a mean 5.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 5.4 Paired t-test 5.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 5.6 Reclassify a variable 5.7 GUIDED QUESTIONS 5.8 CHALLENGES

Software Lesson 5 Hypothesis Test for a Mean in R 5.1 Goal of Software Lesson 5 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to obtain test statistics and -values for carrying out a hypothesis test for a mean, obtain inferential results (e.g., CIs and p-values) for a mean difference when the data are paired, and reclassify a variable (continuous or categorical). Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) 5.2 Hypothesis test for a mean In a previous software lesson, you learned how to estimate a population mean by computing a confidence interval for that parameter. In that lesson, you estimated the population mean weight change from baseline to final visit after starting treatment. Now suppose instead of estimating the population mean, you want to test a particular claim about the population mean. The LHS variable that will be the focus in this section is the same variable you obtained an estimate for in the previous software lesson: the variable for weight change (kg) from baseline to 5th annual visit, wgtchg05. The question that you will be trying to answer here is: Was there a change in the population mean weight between baseline and the final visit after starting treatment? (i.e., \(\mu = 0\)) To carry out a hypothesis test for a mean (when Central Limit Theorem holds), use the t.test() function, specifying the variable of interest, the confidence level (e.g., conf.level=0.95), the appropriate null value (e.g., mu=0), and the appropriate alternative hypothesis (e.g., alternative="two.sided"): t.test(lhs$wgtchg05, conf.level=0.95, mu=0, alternative="two.sided") The t.test() function outputs both a hypothesis test for a mean (t-test statistic, degrees of freedom, and p-value) as well as the \(C\%\) confidence interval for a mean, where \(C\%\) is the defined confidence level. The default/pre-set values for the arguments in the t.test() function are conf.level=0.95, mu=0 (i.e., \(\mu=0\)), and alternative="two.sided". If those are the values that you need for your test, then you can leave these arguments out of the function. Try it out! t.test(lhs$wgtchg05) However, if you have a different confidence level, null value, or alternative hypothesis direction, then you need to include those additional arguments in the function. Plausible values that might be used to modify the confidence level include 0.90 (when you want a \(90\%\) CI) or 0.99 (when you want a \(99\%\) CI). The null value that you input into the mu=value argument depends on the value specified in the null hypothesis for the scenario. Lastly, the other two options that can be used in the alternative= argument are "less" and "greater", depending on how the alternative hypothesis is specified for the scenario. HYPOTHESIS TEST FOR A MEAN Purpose: To evaluate a particular claim about the population mean by using our sample data as evidence. For additional R resources on carrying out a hypothesis test for a mean, see: One-Sample T-test in R [STHDA]. t-tests for means of measurement outcomes [BU-SPH]. Performing a one-sample t-test in R [Instant R]. 5.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 5.3.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the sample mean weight change between baseline to 5th annual visit? 5.3.0.0.2 Question 2 What is the test statistic and the degrees of freedom from the t-test that tested whether the population mean weight change between baseline to 5th annual visit was different from 0? 5.3.0.0.3 Question 3 What is the resulting p-value from the t-test? See 14.12 for answers 5.4 Paired t-test In the LHS study, which was a smoking intervention study, the researchers could be interested in seeing if there was a change in salivary cotinine between the 5th (and last) annual visit (COT5) and baseline (COT0), regardless of the intervention group. This situation can be viewed as a paired data situation because there is a “pre” measurement/variable (baseline) and a “post” measurement/variable (5th visit). The following would be their research question: Is the population mean change in salivary cotinine between baseline to 5th annual visit different from 0? To answer the research question, you would compute a confidence interval to estimate the population mean change and also carry out a hypothesis test for the population mean change. Both of these can be calculated by using the t.test() function (provided that the assumptions for the t-test are valid for the situation). To carry out a hypothesis test for a mean difference (paired data), there are two options available. In the first option, you would include both variables (pre and post) in the t.test() function but also include an additional argument telling R that the data is paired. In the second option, you would create a new variable of the difference and then use that new variable in the t.test() function. Option #1 To carry out a hypothesis test for a mean difference (paired data), use the t.test() function, specifying the two variables of interest, the confidence level (e.g., conf.level=0.95), the appropriate null value (e.g., mu=0), the appropriate alternative hypothesis (e.g., alternative="two.sided"), and the paired nature of the variables (e.g., paired=TRUE): t.test(lhs$COT5, lhs$COT0, conf.level=0.95, mu=0, alternative="two.sided", paired=TRUE) The output for the paired t-test will look very similar to the output when you used t.test() to obtain inferential results for a single mean. Also notice that all of the usual arguments that you learned previously also apply here (e.g., conf.level=, mu=, alternative=). Option #2 To carry out a hypothesis test for a mean difference (paired data), first, create a new variable in the data set (e.g., lhs$cot5diff), followed by the assignment operator (<-), and then take the difference between the two paired variables. Then, use the t.test() function, specifying the variable of interest, the confidence level (e.g., conf.level=0.95), the appropriate null value (e.g., mu=0), and the appropriate alternative hypothesis (e.g., alternative="two.sided"): ####Creating the change variable lhs$cot5diff<-lhs$COT5-lhs$COT0 ####Carrying out the one-sample t-test on the change variable t.test(lhs$cot5diff, conf.level=0.95, mu=0, alternative="two.sided") PAIRED T-TEST Purpose: To make an inference (CI or hypothesis testing) about the population mean change when there is paired data (e.g., pre/post measurements on an individual). For additional R resources on carrying out a hypothesis test for a mean difference (paired data), see: Confidence Interval for a Mean Difference, Paired Samples [BU-SPH]. The Paired Samples t-test [BU-SPH]. Paired Samples t-test in R [STHDA]. 5.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 5.5.0.0.1 Question 4 What is the mean value for the difference between the salivary cotinine level at the 5th visit and the salivary cotinine level at baseline? Which visit had the higher salivary cotinine level, on average? 5.5.0.0.2 Question 5 If the significance level is 0.05 and the test is two-tailed, do the arguments of conf.level=0.95 and alternative="two.sided" need to be specified in the t.test() function? Why or why not? [Hint: Try running the code above with and without these arguments.] 5.5.0.0.3 Question 6 What is the \(95\%\) confidence interval for the population mean change in salivary cotinine between baseline to 5th annual visit? 5.5.0.0.4 Question 7 What is the test statistic and the degrees of freedom from the t-test that tested whether population mean change in salivary cotinine between baseline to 5th annual visit was different from 0? What is the resulting p-value from that test? 5.5.0.0.5 Question 8 Why was the paired=TRUE argument not specified in the following line of code: t.test(lhs$cot5diff, conf.level=0.95, mu=0, alternative="two.sided")? See 14.12 for answers 5.6 Reclassify a variable There are situations where you might need to reclassify a numerical variable as a categorical one, or recode categories within a categorical variable. 5.6.1 Reclassify numerical variable as categorical variable Let’s learn how to reclassify a numerical variable from the LHS data set (breathing test value at the 3rd annual visit, FEVFVC3) into a categorical variable. Suppose that you want to turn this variable into a binary variable: normal lung function and abnormal lung function. Based on guidelines, an individual’s lung function is considered “normal” if the ratio between FEV1 and FVC is equal to or greater than \(70\%\) (i.e., \(\frac{FEV_1}{FVC} \ge 70\%\) ). Conversely, an individual’s lung function is considered “abnormal” if the ratio is less than \(70\%\) (i.e., \(\frac{FEV_1}{FVC} < 70\%\)). To reclassify a numerical variable into a categorical one, create a new variable in the data set (e.g., lhs$lung.fxn), followed by the assignment operator (<-), and then use the cut() function, specifying the variable of interest to be converted, the cut-off values in the numerical variable (e.g., breaks=c(0, 69.99999999, 100)), and the labels for the categories (e.g. labels=c("abnormal","normal")): lhs$lung.fxn<-cut(lhs$FEVFVC3, breaks=c(0, 69.99999999, 100), labels=c("abnormal","normal")) The breaks= argument specifies the cut-offs for how you want to recode the variable. To figure out how many values should be included in the breaks= argument: If you are creating two categories, you would specify three break values. If you are creating three categories, you would specify four break values. etc. In the FEVFVC3 variable, the minimum value for the ratio is 0 and the maximum is 100. Since you want 70 or greater to be labeled as “normal”, you should set the cut-off mark in the code as a number less than but very close to 70 (e.g., 69.99999999). That way, the abnormal group consists of ratio values between 0 and 69.99999999 and the normal group consists of ratio values between 70 and 100. R TIP It’s good practice to view the first few rows of the data to make sure that the software carried out the reclassification as you intended. To refresh you memory on how to view the first few rows, review Software Lesson 1. 5.6.2 Recode categories in a categorical variable Suppose you want to recode a categorical variable so that “Yes” is labeled as 1 and “No” is labeled as 0. (Note: The 0/1 dummy coding is typically how binary variables are denoted, but it is not always the case.) In the LHS data set, the f060pipe variable, which represents smoking a pipe at the 1st annual visit, is coded not as one would expect: 1=Yes and 2=No. Let’s recode this variable so that 1=Yes and 0=No.  Option #1: Using mapvalues() function To recode categories within a categorical variable, create a new variable in the data set, followed by the assignment operator (<-), and then use the mapvalues() function from the {plyr} package, specifying the variable of interest to be converted, what the original values were (e.g., from=c(1, 2)), and how to recode the values (e.g., to=c(1, 0)): ############# # Run the install.packages() function only once per package ############# install.packages("plyr") ###Access the package using the library() function library(plyr) ###Recode categories using the mapvalues() function lhs$pipe1<-mapvalues(lhs$f060pipe, from=c(1, 2), to = c(1, 0)) The order of the values in the from= and to= arguments matter. The first element in from= corresponds to the first element in to=, the second element to the second element, and so on. R TIP The c() function has been used a few times in this software lesson. Recall that the c() function is used when there are multiple values that have to be specified. Option #2: Using ifelse() function To recode categories within a categorical variable, create a new variable in the data set, followed by the assignment operator (<-), and then use the ifelse() function, specifying the variable to be converted and the condition to check, then the new value if the condition is true, and lastly the new value if the condition is false: ###Recode categories as values lhs$pipe10<-ifelse(lhs$f060pipe == 2, 0, 1) ###Recode categories as text lhs$pipe10f<-ifelse(lhs$f060pipe == 2, "No", "Yes") R TIP As before, it’s good practice to check to make sure the recoding is done correctly. One way to do this when recoding categories of a categorical variable is examine a 2x2 table of the two variables: table(data$new_var, data$old_var). RECLASSIFY A VARIABLE Purpose: To turn a numerical variable into a categorical one, or recode categories within a categorical variable. For additional R resources on reclassifying a variable, see: Recoding Data [Cookbook for R]. 5.7 GUIDED QUESTIONS 5.7.0.0.1 Question 9 Why are quotes placed around the labels abnormal and normal in the cut() function? 5.7.0.0.2 Question 10 If you were reclassifying a numerical variable into five categories, how many values would you need to specify in the breaks= argument? 5.7.0.0.3 Question 11 What is the name of the new variable in the LHS data set when the categories of the variable f060pipe was recoded? See 14.12 for answers 5.8 CHALLENGES 5.8.0.0.1 Question 12 There is evidence that on average, people gain weight after quitting smoking. Suppose the researchers wanted to test whether there was a increase in mean weight change one year after starting treatment (wgtchg01). Carry out the appropriate hypothesis test for the population mean of this variable. 5.8.0.0.2 Question 13 Reclassify the numerical variable for weight change (kg) from baseline to first annual visit, wgtchg01, as a categorical variable with three categories: lost wt,no change, and gained wt. See 14.12.0.1 for answers

Software Lesson 6 Inferential Methods for Comparing Means & More Summarizing Numerical Data in R 6.1 Goal of Software Lesson 6 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to obtain inferential results (e.g., CIs and p-values) for comparing two means: two-sample t-test and Mann-Whitney test/Wilcoxon rank-sum test. summarize a numerical variable by group (descriptively and graphically), and transform a numerical variable in a data set. Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) 6.2 Two-sample t-test Suppose that the LHS researchers wanted to investigate whether there was a difference in expired carbon monoxide (CO) level at baseline (CO0) between the male and female smokers (AGENDER). Because there are two separate, independent groups and you want to compare them on a numerical variable, this would be a situation for carrying out a two-sample t-test. The following would be their research question: Is there a difference in the population mean CO level at baseline between the male and female smokers? To answer the research question, you would compute a confidence interval to estimate the difference in the population means between males and females and also carry out a hypothesis test for a difference in two population means. Both of these can be calculated by using the t.test() function (provided that the assumptions for the two-sample t-test are valid for the situation). To carry out a hypothesis test for comparing two means (two independent groups), use the t.test() function, specifying the numerical variable first, followed by the ~ symbol (NOT the , symbol), and then the grouping variable, as well as all of the other potential arguments of the function, as deemed necessary (e.g., conf.level=, mu=, alternative=, var.equal=FALSE for unequal variance): t.test(lhs$CO0~lhs$AGENDER, conf.level=0.95, mu=0, alternative="two.sided", var.equal=FALSE) The output for the two-sample t-test will look very similar to the output when you used t.test() to obtain inferential results for a single mean or mean difference. Also notice that all of the usual arguments that you learned in a previous lesson also apply here (e.g., conf.level=, mu=, alternative=). The new argument, var.equal=, is, by default, set as var.equal=FALSE, or unequal variance assumption. TWO-SAMPLE T-TEST Purpose: To make an inference (CI or hypothesis testing) about the difference in population means between two groups. For additional R resources on carrying out a hypothesis test for comparing two means, see: The Independent Samples T-Test to Compare Two Means [BU-SPH]. Two-Sample T-Test in R: Independent Groups [MarinStatsLectures (youtube video)]. Unpaired Two-Samples T-Test in R [STHDA]. 6.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 6.3.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the difference in the two mean CO values at baseline between male and female smokers? Which group had the higher CO baseline mean? 6.3.0.0.2 Question 2 What is the \(95\%\) confidence interval for the difference in the two population mean CO values at baseline between male and female smokers? 6.3.0.0.3 Question 3 What is the test statistic and the degrees of freedom from the two-sample t-test? What is the resulting p-value from that test? See 14.13 for answers 6.4 Mann-Whitney test (aka Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test) Now suppose that the normality assumption was not met for carrying out a two-sample t-test. When this happens, you can use a nonparametric method that doesn’t assume normality: the Mann-Whitney test, also known as the Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test. For the LHS study, suppose that the researchers were interested in whether there was a difference in salivary cotinine level at the 4th annual visit (COT4) between the male and female smokers (AGENDER). Also suppose that the conditions were not met to carry out a two-sample t-test. The Mann-Whitney test could be to used as an alternative approach for this situation. Using the Mann-Whitney test, the following would be their research question: Is there a difference in the median salivary cotinine levels at the 4th annual visit between the males and females? Note: For this situation, the sample size is large enough that you don’t have to be concerned about the normality assumption (i.e., the CLT will hold because n is “large enough”). But let’s pretend that the normality assumption was an issue and another method was necessary. To carry out Mann-Whitney test for comparing two medians (two independent groups), use the wilcox.test() function, specifying the numerical variable first, followed by the ~ symbol (NOT the , symbol), and then the grouping variable, as well as all of the other potential arguments of the function, as deemed necessary (e.g., conf.int=, conf.level=, mu=, alternative=): wilcox.test(lhs$COT4~lhs$AGENDER, conf.int=TRUE, conf.level=0.95, alternative="two.sided", mu=0) The output provides a test statistic and a p-value from the test results. MANN-WHITNEY TEST Purpose: To make an inference (hypothesis testing) about the difference in population medians between two groups. This is the nonparametric version to the two-sample t-test, if the normality condition wasn’t met. For additional R resources on carrying out a Mann-Whitney test, see: Mann-Whitney-Wilcoxon Test [R Tutorial]. Unpaired Two-Samples Wilcoxon Test in R [STHDA]. 6.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 6.5.0.0.1 Question 4 What is the test statistic from the Mann-Whitney test? What is the resulting p-value from that test? See 14.13 for answers 6.6 Summarizing numerical variables by group In a previous lab, you learned how to create a graphical displays (e.g., boxplot, histogram) and calculate summary measures (e.g., mean, median, sd) for a single numerical variable. However, it may be of interest to compare groups on the numerical variable. This can be done with functions that summarize a variable by group or by multivariate plots (e.g., histograms by group, side-by-side boxplots). The two LHS variables that will be the focus in this section are the age variable, age, and the intervention group variable, alphagroup. 6.6.1 Summary measures of numerical variable by group There are two ways to calculate summary measures of a numerical variable by group. The first option is to use the tapply() function, which allows you to calculate single measures at a time, and the second option is to use the describeBy() function, which calculates multiple summary measures at once. Option #1 To compute summary measures of a numerical variable by group, use the tapply() function, specifying the numerical variable in the first argument (e.g., lhs$age), followed by the grouping variable (e.g., lhs$alphagroup), and then adding the function of the summary measure that you want computed (e.g., median) in the last argument: tapply(lhs$age, lhs$alphagroup, median) Other summary measure functions that may be applied in the last argument of the tapply() function include mean,sd, IQR, max, and min. R TIP Recall that the summary measure functions above will return \(NA\) for any variable that has missing values. In those situations, you will need to include a na.rm=TRUE as the last argument in the function (e.g., tapply(object$num.var, object$grp.var, function, na.rm=TRUE)). For example, the LHS variable for salivary cotinine at 4th annual visit ( COT4) has missing values. To compute the mean for this variable by intervention group, you would type the following code: tapply(lhs$COT4, lhs$alphagroup, mean, na.rm=TRUE). Option #2 To compute summary measures of a numerical variable by group, use the describeBy() function from the {psych} package, specifying the numerical variable in the first argument (e.g., lhs$age), followed by the grouping variable (e.g., lhs$alphagroup) in the last argument [Note: You can also compute IQR and quantiles (e.g., Q1 and Q3) by adding additional (optional) arguments, IQR=TRUE and quant=c(0.25, 0.75)]: ###Access the package using the library() function library(psych) ###Calculate multiple summary statistics by group using the describeBy() function describeBy(lhs$age,lhs$alphagroup, IQR=TRUE, quant=c(.25,.75)) The describeBy() function is related to the describe() function you learned in a previous lab. It outputs the 12 summary statistics that describe() does, plus the three additional ones (IQR, Q1 (Q0.25), and Q3 (Q0.75)), but computes them by group. 6.6.2 Graphical displays of numerical variable by group The two useful graphical displays that are used to compare the distributions of a numerical variable by group are the side-by-side boxplot and multiple histograms by group. Side-by-side boxplot To create a side-by-side boxplot of a numerical variable by group, use the boxplot() function, specifying the numerical variable first, followed by the ~ sign (NOT the , symbol), and then the grouping variable next, and other arguments if you wish to customize the bar plot (e.g., main= for the plot title): boxplot(lhs$age~lhs$alphagroup, main="Boxplot of LHS Ages by \n Intervention Group") Multiple histograms To create histograms of a numerical variable by group, use the histogram() function from the {lattice} package, specifying the the ~ sign first, followed by the numerical variable and the | symbol, and then the grouping variable, and other arguments if you wish to customize the bar plot (e.g., xlab= for the x-axis label, main= for the plot title): ############# # Run the install.packages() function only once per package ############# install.packages("lattice") ###Access the package using the library() function library(lattice) ###Create multiple histograms using the histogram() function histogram(~lhs$age | lhs$alphagroup, xlab="Age", main="Histograms of Age by \n Intervention Group") **SUMMARIZING A NUMERICAL VARIABLE BY GROUP* Purpose: To compare groups on their distributions of a numerical variable using summary measures and graphical representations. For additional R resources on summarizing a numerical variable by group, see: R Function of the Day: tapply [R-bloggers]. Descriptive Stats with describe() and describeBy() [DuckLabs]. Box Plots - R Base Graphs[STHDA]. R Box Plot [Data Mentor]. Using lattice’s histogram() [tigerstats]. Lattice Histogram in R [Tutorial Getaway]. 6.7 GUIDED QUESTIONS 6.7.0.0.1 Question 5 What are the median ages for the three intervention groups? What about the mean ages? The SD of the ages? 6.7.0.0.2 Question 6 Compare and contrast the three distributions of age by intervention group status (e.g., shape, center, spread). Informally, based on your comparison, does it appear that the groups are different from each other? Explain. See 14.13 for answers 6.8 Transforming a numerical variable In a previous lab, you created a new variable in a data set of the difference between two variables for each observation. Recall that do this, the beginning of the code looked like dataset$newvariablename<-. Now, you are going to learn how to transform an existing numerical variable into a new one, using the log-transformation. For example, suppose you want to log-transform a skewed variable, such as the LHS variable of average number of cigarettes per day at 5th annual visit (AV5CIGS). To log-transform a numerical variable in the data set, create a new variable in the data set (e.g., lhs$logAvgCigs.5), followed by the assignment operator (<-), and then use the log() function, specifying the variable that you want to take a log of (e.g., lhs$AV5CIGS): lhs$logAvgCigs.5<-log(lhs$AV5CIGS) Now, when the head() function is called, you will see that R attached a new column at the end of the data set (i.e., logAvgCigs.5). You can now apply any of the functions you have learned on this new numerical variable (e.g., summary(lhs$logAvgCigs.5), hist(lhs$logAvgCigs.5)). R TIP When naming new objects or variables in R, don’t use underscores (\(\_\)) or hyphens (\(-\)). Also, don’t start the names with a number. The preferred format is all lower case letters and words separated with dots (variable.name), but variableName is also acceptable, as are title case (VariableName). **TRANSFORMING A NUMERICAL VARIABLE* Purpose: To transform an existing numerical variable into a new one, using the log-transformation. For additional R resources on transforming a numerical variable in a data set, see: Creating New Variables [Quick-R]. Creating New Variables in R [BU]. 6.9 GUIDED QUESTIONS 6.9.0.0.1 Question 7 Use the head() function on the lhs data set. What is the value in the first row of the log-transformed AV5CIGS variable (logAvgCigs.5)? See 14.13 for answers 6.10 CHALLENGES The Challenge this week will force you to review everything you have learned thus far in the software lesson documents. Suppose you were interested in whether there was a difference between males and females on change in cigarette use from beginning to end of the study. The following would be your research question: Is the a difference in the population mean change in cigarette use (pre to post) between males and females? To start, you will need to create one new variable for the numerical outcome. And then you can use that newly created variable to carry out the appropriate analyses. 6.10.0.0.1 Question 8 Create a new variable in the data set that computes the difference between the “pre” cigarette use (f10cigs) and “post” cigarette use (AV5CIGS). Name this new variable cigdiff. 6.10.0.0.2 Question 9 Compute summary statistics for the difference in cigarette use (cigdiff) by sex (AGENDER). 6.10.0.0.3 Question 10 Create a histogram for the difference in cigarette use (cigdiff) by sex (AGENDER). Also create a side-by-side boxplot for the difference in cigarette use (cigdiff) by sex (AGENDER). 6.10.0.0.4 Question 11 Carry out the appropriate inferential method for answering the research question. See 14.13.0.1 for answers
