Software Lesson 7 Power & Sample Size Estimation in R 7.1 Goal of Software Lesson 7 7.2 Power and Sample Size Calculations 7.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 7.4 GUIDED QUESTIONS 7.5 CHALLENGES

Software Lesson 7 Power & Sample Size Estimation in R 7.1 Goal of Software Lesson 7 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to estimate a sample size for a study comparing two means by specifying the power value, the significance level, and the estimated effect size, and estimate the power of a study comparing two means by specifying the sample size, the significance level, and the estimated effect size. 7.2 Power and Sample Size Calculations An important step in doing research is determining the appropriate sample size for a given study. When figuring this out, you must consider Type I error, power, and cost. Two particular cases will be described in this lesson: Compute the appropriate sample size given a specified power. Calculate the power given a specific sample size. Note: Most of the time, research deals with comparing groups. Thus, this lesson will focus on the two-sample situation comparing means, but it is possible to compute power and sample size for the two other situations you have learned about in this class: a single variable (i.e., one-sample) or for paired data. The following four values have a close relationship with one another: Sample size Effect size (i.e., quantifying the difference between two groups) Significance level, \(\alpha\) Power If you know three of the four, you can determine the fourth quantity. 7.2.1 Sample size estimation Suppose researchers want to estimate the sample size for a study where they are comparing two group means. They want to have \(80\%\) power, assume an effect size of 0.4, and set the significance level at 0.05. To calculate a sample size estimate prior to a study that will compare two group means, use the pwr.t.test() function from the {pwr} package, specifying effect size (d=), power (power=), significance level (sig.level=), and type of t-test (type=): ############# # Run the install.packages() function once per package ############# install.packages("pwr") ###Access the package using the library() function library(pwr) ####Estimate the sample size pwr.t.test(d=0.4, power=0.8, sig.level = 0.05, type = "two.sample") The output will provide you with an estimated sample size (n =) as well as each of the inputs that you provided in the pwr.t.test() function (i.e., d =, sig.level =, power =, and alternative =). For t-tests, the effect size is computed using the following formula: \(d = \frac{|\mu_1 - \mu_2|}{\sigma}\), where \(\mu_1\) = mean of “group 1”, \(\mu_2\) = mean of “group 2”, and \(\sigma\) = standard deviation. One guideline for how to evaluate effect size values (e.g., d) is: 0.2 is small, 0.5 is medium, and 0.8 is large. For more information about effect sizes, see the following website: It’s the Effect Size, Stupid: What effect size is and why it is important. The type= argment is set at "two.sample" to indicate that you are comparing two (independent) groups. The other two options for the type= argument are "paired" (when you are comparing a mean change from paired data) and "one.sample" (when you are testing a single mean). Lastly, there is another argument, alternative=, that can be specified in the function. This argument indicates the direction of alternative hypothesis: two-sided, greater than, or less than. The default is a two-sided (alternative = "two.sided"), but you could also set the alternative to be "less" or "greater". SAMPLE SIZE ESTIMATION Purpose: To calculate the optimum number of participants required in a sample so that true inferences about the population are able to be made based on the results that were obtained. This calculation should be done prior to conducting a study. For more information about sample size estimation, see this article: Sample Size Calculation For additional R resources on sample size estimation, see: Power Analysis [Quick-R]. R-tutorial: Power and Sample Size Calculation [Kansas University]. 7.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 7.3.0.0.1 Question 1 What was the estimated sample size that was calculated for each group when the effect size was set at 0.4, power at \(80\%\), and significance level at 0.05? Round your answer up. See 14.14 for answers 7.3.1 Power analysis Now, let’s estimate the power of the study, given sample sizes (equal or unequal sizes), an assumed effect size, and a significance level. Example #1: Equal sample sizes A researcher was only able to obtain 41 participants in each group (sample size is equal for both groups). Given that value, she’s interested in figuring out the potential power of her study (Note: This is still prior to completing the the study). She assumes the effect size will be 0.55 and sets the significance level at 0.05. To calculate power with equal sample sizes in two group means, use the pwr.t.test() function from the {pwr} package, specifying sample size of each group (n=), effect size (d=), significance level (sig.level=), and type of t-test (type=): ####Calculating power, equal sample sizes pwr.t.test(n=41, d=0.55, sig.level = 0.05, type = "two.sample") You will see similar output as in the example for estimating sample size, but this time, you will be looking at the estimated power value (power =) based on the given inputs. Example #2: Unequal sample sizes Now, instead, suppose the researcher had unequal sample sizes, 16 in one group and 21 in the other group. Given those values, she’s interested in figuring out the potential power of her study (Note: This is still prior to completing the the study). She assumes the same effect size and significance level as in Example #1 (0.55 and 0.05, respectively). To calculate power with unequal sample sizes in two group means, use the pwr.t2n.test() function from the {pwr} package, specifying sample size of each group (n1= and n2=), effect size (d=), and significance level (sig.level=): ####Calculating power, unequal sample sizes pwr.t2n.test(n1=16, n2=21, d=0.55, sig.level = 0.05) Notice that you didn’t have to specify the type of t-test when you use the pwr.t2n.test() function. This is because the function is specifically for two-sample t-test with unequal sample sizes. Again, you will see similar output as in the last two cases, except now you have n1 = and n2 = to indicate the two different sample sizes. Similar to Example #1, you will be looking at the estimated power value (power =) based on the given inputs. POWER ANALYSIS Purpose: To calculate the power value given a particular sample size, effect size, and significance level. It is recommended that this calculation be done prior to conducting a study. Confidence intervals and effect sizes should be calculated after study results are obtained instead of post-hoc power calculations. For more information about power, see this article: Introduction to Power Analysis Note that this article presents a lot of information but does a nice job of explaining power analyses. For additional R resources on power analysis, see: Power Analysis [Quick-R]. R-Tutorial: Power and Sample Size Calculation [Kansas University]. 7.4 GUIDED QUESTIONS 7.4.0.0.1 Question 2 What was the calculated power when the sample size was 41, the effect size was 0.55, and the significance level was 0.05? 7.4.0.0.2 Question 3 What was the calculated power when the sample sizes were 16 and 21, the effect size was 0.55, and significance level was 0.05? See 14.14 for answers 7.5 CHALLENGES Let’s explore how effect size, power, and significance level affects the estimated sample size. 7.5.0.0.1 Question 4 Choose three other effect sizes (e.g., 0.1, 0.6, 1.2) to explore how effect size affects the estimated sample size (with power = 0.8 and significance level = 0.05 each time). 7.5.0.0.2 Question 5 Choose three other power values (e.g., 0.7, 0.85, 0.95) to explore how power affects the estimated sample size (with effect size = 0.4 and significance level = 0.05 each time). 7.5.0.0.3 Question 6 Choose three other significance levels (e.g., 0.01, 0.05, 0.1) to explore how significance level affects the estimated sample size (with power = 0.8 and effect size = 0.4 each time). 7.5.0.0.4 Question 7 Choose three other sample sizes (e.g., 10, 50, 134) to explore how sample size affects power (with effect size = 0.4 and significance level = 0.05 each time). 7.5.0.0.5 Question 8 Choose three other effect sizes (e.g., 0.1, 0.6, 1.2) to explore how effect size affects power (with sample size = 40 and significance level = 0.05 each time). 7.5.0.0.6 Question 9 Choose three other significance levels (e.g., 0.01, 0.05, 0.1) to explore how significance level affects power (with sample size = 40 and effect size = 0.4 each time). See 14.14.0.1 for answers

Software Lesson 9 ANOVA in R 9.1 Goal of Software Lesson 9 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to obtain inferential results for comparing three or more means, and obtain post-hoc results while properly adjusting for multiple comparisons. Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) 9.2 ANOVA Thus far, you have learned inferential methods for one mean (one-sample or paired situation) and for comparing two means (two-sample situation). However, there are situations when researchers want to compare means between three or more groups. When this is the case, one would use ANOVA to compare the three (or more) group means, simultaneously. In the LHS study, the researchers investigated three interventions: smoking intervention plus atrovent, smoking intervention plus placebo, and usual care (i.e., no active intervention). The researchers were most likely interested in how those interventions compared to one another on a variety of outcomes (e.g., cigarette and tobacco habits over duration of the study, weight change). Suppose that the researchers were interested in the effect of intervention group (alphagroup) on weight change at the 5th annual visit (wgtchg05). The following would be their research question: Is there a difference in average weight change at the 5th annual visit between the three LHS intervention groups? When carrying out ANOVA in R, you first need to save the ANOVA results from the aov() function into an object (e.g., myANOVA) and then use that object in the summary() function to structure the ANOVA results into a meaningful table. Step 1: To carry out an ANOVA comparing three or more groups, first, name the object something meaningful (e.g., wgt.ANOVA) and then use the assignment operator (<-) to assign the results from the aov() function to that object. Then, use the aov() function, specifying the numerical variable first and the grouping variable second (with a ~ in between): wgt.ANOVA<-aov(lhs$wgtchg05~lhs$alphagroup) Step 2: To obtain the ANOVA results, use the summary() function on the object that contains the ANOVA results from the aov() function: summary(wgt.ANOVA) The results are presented in an ANOVA table. This table provides statistics regarding the variability between groups (first row) and the variability within groups (second row), as well as the usual hypothesis test information (i.e., test statistic (i.e., F value), p-value (i.e., Pr(>F))). ANOVA Purpose: To make an inference about whether the means differ between three or more groups. For additional R resources on ANOVA, see: One factor ANOVA comparing means across several groups [BU]. One-Way ANOVA Test in R [STHDA]. 9.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 9.3.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the value of the F-statistic from the ANOVA test? What are the degrees of freedom that was used in this test for the F-distribution? What is the p-value? See 14.16 for answers 9.4 Multiple Comparisons If the results from ANOVA are statistically significant, then you can conclude that there is evidence that at least one of the group means is different from the others. But then this begs the questions of “well, how different are they?” and “which means are different from the others?”. This is where post-hoc (meaning “after the event”) analyses come into play. Post-hoc analyses following ANOVA allow us to compare the means of each possible pair of groups (e.g., group A compared to group B, group A compared to group C, group B compared to group C). However, there is a limitation to carrying out multiple tests simultaneously: increasing the Type I error rate, called multiple comparisons issue (see Beware of Multiple Comparisons). Many methods have been developed to tackle this issue, including Tukey’s HSD method and the Bonferroni method, to name a few. You can think of these corrections as running all possible two-sample t-tests for which the results have been corrected. Tukey’s HSD and Bonferroni will be presented in this lesson. 9.4.0.1 Tukey’s HSD To carry out Tukey’s multiple comparisons method, use the TukeyHSD() function, specifying the object that contains the ANOVA results from the aov() function: TukeyHSD(wgt.ANOVA) This function provides a table of information on the pairwise comparisons. Each row presents a pairwise group comparison. The first column (diff) provides the difference in means between the two groups. The second (lwr) and third (upr) columns provide the lower and upper values of a \(95\%\) adjusted confidence interval for the difference in the two means. And the final column (p adj) provides an adjusted p-value testing the difference in the two means. 9.4.1 Bonferroni To carry out Bonferroni multiple comparisons method, use the pairwise.t.test() function, specifying the numerical variable, the grouping variable, and the particular method for adjusting fo multiple comparisons (p.adj= "bonferroni): pairwise.t.test(lhs$wgtchg05, lhs$alphagroup, p.adj = "bonferroni") This function provides a table of adjusted Bonferroni p-values for comparing each of the groups to each other. Note that the p-values have already been adjusted for you, so you would compare these p-values to the standard \(\alpha\) level. If the p-values had not been adjusted for you, then you would have needed to compare the [unadjusted] p-values to the adjusted \(\alpha\) level using the Bonferroni approach (i.e., \(\frac{\alpha}{\# pairs}\)). MULTIPLE COMPARISONS Purpose: To adjust for multiple comparisons when carrying out post-hoc analyses following ANOVA. For additional R resources on multiple comparisons, see: How can I do post-hoc pairwise comparisons in R? [UCLA]. 9.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 9.5.0.0.1 Question 2 What is the difference in the two mean weight changes between UC and SI-A groups? Which group had the higher weight change, on average? 9.5.0.0.2 Question 3 What is the \(95\%\) adjusted confidence interval for the difference in the two population mean weight changes between UC and SI-A groups? What is the resulting adjusted p-value for that same comparison using the Tukey’s HSD method? 9.5.0.0.3 Question 4 What is the adjusted p-value for comparing the SI-P and UC group means using the Bonferroni method? See 14.16 for answers 9.6 CHALLENGE QUESTIONS Similar to a previous software lesson, the challenge section this week will force you to incorporate everything you have learned thus far in the software lesson documents. Suppose you were interested in the effect of the intervention groups on change in cigarette use from beginning to end of the study. The following would be your research question: Is the a difference in the population mean change in cigarette use (pre to post) between the intervention groups?} Recall that the numerical outcome is the change in cigarette use from beginning to end of study (visit in year 5). To start, you will need to create one new variable for the numerical outcome. And then you can use the newly created variable to carry out the appropriate analyses. 9.6.0.0.1 Question 5 Create a new variable in the data set that computes the difference between the “pre” cigarette use (f10cigs) and “post” cigarette use (AV5CIGS). Name this new variable cigdiff. 9.6.0.0.2 Question 6 Compute summary statistics for the difference in cigarette use (cigdiff) by intervention group (alphagroup). 9.6.0.0.3 Question 7 Create a histogram for the difference in cigarette use (cigdiff) by intervention group (alphagroup). Also create a side-by-side boxplot for the difference in cigarette use (cigdiff) by intervention group (alphagroup). 9.6.0.0.4 Question 8 Carry out the appropriate inferential method for answering the research question. If the result is statistically significant, proceed to perform the appropriate post-hoc analyses to answer the research question. See 14.16.0.1 for answers

Software Lesson 10 Relative Risk and Odds Ratios & More Summarizing Categorical Data in R 10.1 Goal of Software Lesson 10 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to summarize two categorical variables (descriptively and graphically), including 2x2 tables and plots, calculate point and interval estimates for comparing two categorical variables (i.e., risk difference, relative risk, and odds ratio), and modify labels of a categorical variable. Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) 10.2 Comparing two categorical variables Suppose that the LHS researchers were interested in estimating the relative risk of using nicotine gum at the 1st annual visit (AV1GUM), comparing females to males (AGENDER). This is a situation for using techniques that compare two groups on a categorical variable. The following would be their research question: What is an estimate of the population difference in risks/odds of using nicotine gum at 1 year after starting treatment between the sexes? To answer the research question, you could compute and estimate the following summary measures: risk difference (RD), relative risk (RR), or odds ratio (OR), depending on the study design. However, before you compute these values, you should first examine the relationship between two categorical variables using 2x2 (or contingency) tables and stacked or side-by-side bar plots. 10.3 Summarizing two categorical variables In a previous software lesson, you learned how to create a table and bar plot for a single categorical variable. However, it may be of interest to examine the relationship between two categorical variables. This can be done with 2x2 (or contingency) tables and by stacked or side-by-side bar plots. 10.3.1 2x2 tables To create a 2x2 table of counts, use the table() function, specifying the two variables of interest: table(lhs$AGENDER, lhs$AV1GUM) The first variable in the argument (e.g., lhs$AGENDER) is placed in the rows and the second variable in the argument (e.g., lhs$AV1GUM) is placed in the columns. If you want the 2x2 table to be proportions rather than counts, there are two ways to do this. The first option, prop.table(), allows you to create three different kinds of 2x2 tables, depending if you want proportions by total (cell value divided by total), proportions by row (cell value divided by row total), or proportions by columns (cell value divide by column total). The second option, CrossTable(), gives you all of that information in a single table. Option #1: prop.table() for table of proportions When creating a 2x2 table of proportions using the prop.table() function, you first need to save the 2x2 table of counts from the table() function into an object (e.g., mytable) and then use that object in the function for computing table proportions (prop.table()). Step 1: To save the 2x2 table of counts into an object, first, name the object something meaningful (e.g., mytable), and then use the assignment operator (<-) to assign the 2x2 table of counts (e.g., table(lhs$AGENDER, lhs$AV1GUM)) to the object: mytable<-table(lhs$AGENDER, lhs$AV1GUM) Step 2: To create a 2x2 table of proportions, use the prop.table() function, specifying the saved object containing the 2x2 table of counts (e.g., mytable) and other arguments if you want specific proportions (e.g., margin = 1 for proportions by row, margin = 2 for proportions by column): prop.table(mytable) # cell proportions, cell/total prop.table(mytable, margin = 1) # row proportions, cell/row total prop.table(mytable, 2) # column proportions, cell/column total Row proportions and column proportions are helpful when you want to know the proportions given a particular subcategory. For example, based on the output from the row proportions code, you could answer the question of, “what proportion of female participants were using nicotine gum at 1 year after starting treatment?” Option #2: CrossTable() for table of proportions If you are looking for function that provides a multitude of information at once (counts, proportions), then use the CrossTable() function. The author that created this function modeled it after output provided by SAS for 2x2 tables (proc freq task in SAS). To create a 2x2 table with multiple values in each cell, use the CrossTable() function from the {gmodels} package, specifying the two variables of interest: ############# # Run the install.packages() function once per package ############# install.packages("gmodels") ###Access the package using the library() function library(gmodels) ###Calculate multiple summary statistics using the CrossTable() function CrossTable(lhs$AGENDER, lhs$AV1GUM) The values computed by this function include count, Chi-square contribution (ignore for now), proportion of the row, proportion of the column, and proportion of the table for each cell, respectively, for each cell in the table. 10.3.2 Graphical displays of a categorical variable by group After creating a 2x2 table, it might be helpful to visually examine the data via a bar plot. Two types of bar plots include stacked bar plots and side-by-side bar plots. The function is the same as you learned in a previous software lesson, barplot() but now you use your 2x2 table object. Stacked bar plot To create a stacked bar plot of counts by group, use the barplot() function, specifying the saved object containing the 2x2 table of counts (e.g., mytable) and other arguments if you want to customize the bar plot (e.g., main= for the plot title, xlab= for the bar labels, and col= for modifying the bar colors): ###Create the stacked barplot barplot(t(mytable), main="Barplot of Nicotine Gum Usage by Sex", xlab="Sex", col=c("darkblue","green")) ###Add a legend to the barplot legend(x=0.25,y=3000,legend=rownames(t(mytable)), fill=c("darkblue","green"), cex=0.5) Notice that there is a t() around the table object (mytable). This is to transpose the table so that the groups of interest are placed on the x-axis and the fill is the outcome/response of interest. For any plot, you can add a legend using the legend() function to describe features of the plot. The arguments that are included in the above code include: the x- and y-coordinates where the legend will be positioned (x= and y=), the text within the legend (legend=) [Notice again that t() was used for the table to be consistent with how the plot was oriented.], the colors that correspond to the text (fill=), and the size (relative to 1) of the legend box (cex=). Values less than 1 reduce the size of the legend and values greater than 1 increase the size of the legend. Side-by-side bar plot To create a side-by-side bar plot by group, use the barplot() function, specifying the saved object containing the 2x2 table of counts (e.g., mytable), the option for creating side-by-side bars (i.e., beside=TRUE), and other arguments if you want to customize the bar plot (e.g., main= for the plot title, xlab= for the bar labels, and col= for modifying the bar colors): barplot(t(mytable), beside=TRUE, main="Barplot of Nicotine Gum Usage by Sex", xlab="Sex", col=c("darkblue","green")) legend(x=1,y=2500,legend=rownames(t(mytable)), fill=c("darkblue","green"), cex=0.5) R TIP As a reminder, if you want the formal R documentation for a function, type ? before the function name (e.g., ?barplot). This is useful when you want to see what other arguments can be called in the function. Also, if you curious on the names of the colors that are available in R, see the following website: Colors in R. 10.3.3 Risk difference, relative risk, and odds ratio When finding RD, RR, and OR in R, you have to first manually create the 2x2 table before using the function that finds the summary values for comparing categorical variables. When creating this table, it is very important you set up the table correctly in order for the function to work. The first column in the table needs to be the event of interest and the first row needs to be the group of interest. As an example, this is how the table should look: Event Not Event Group 1 a b Group 2 c d Step 1 (skip this step if you only have counts and/or totals for each group and not a entire data set): To create and view a 2x2 table of counts, use the table() function, specifying the two variables of interest, exposure or grouping variable first followed by the response or outcome variable next: table(lhs$AGENDER, lhs$AV1GUM) Now that you have the counts, you can manually create the 2x2 table. However, notice that the table that was produced by the above code has the event of interest (1) in the second column. Therefore, you cannot just copy the counts down in the order that the table() function displays them. You have to be conscious of order when you manually record the values. Step 2: To create a matrix of a 2x2 table of counts, use the matrix() function to create a table of the data, specifying the counts in the c() function in the order of a, b, c, and d (according the table presented earlier), the number of rows in the table (nrow=2), the number of columns in the table (ncol=2), the order to read in the values (byrow=TRUE fills in the values across the rows), and the row names and column names in the table (dimnames=list(c("Row1Name", "Row2Name"), c("Column1Name", "Column2Name")): mytable1<-matrix( c(387,1708,551,2942), #specifying the cell values nrow=2, #specifying the number of rows ncol=2, #specifying the number of columns byrow=TRUE, #create the matrix by rows dimnames=list(c("Female", "Male"), c("Used nicotine gum", "Did not use nicotine gum"))) ####View the table that you just created mytable1 R TIP It’s good practice when creating 2x2 tables to view the table you just created, especially, in this case, to make sure it was entered in correctly. To do this, you just need to type the name of the object that contains the 2x2 table and run that line of code. Step 3: To find estimates (RD, RR, and OR) for comparing categorical variables, use the summary(epi2x2()) functions, where the epi2x2() function is from the {epibasix} package, specifying the saved object containing the 2x2 table of counts (e.g., mytable1): ############# # Run the install.packages() function once per package ############# install.packages("epibasix") ###Access the package using the library() function library(epibasix) ###Calculate multiple summary statistics using the CrossTable() function summary(epi2x2(mytable1)) The output presents you everything that you might be interested in knowing when comparing two categorical variables: 2x2 table, hypothesis testing for two inferential methods (Chi-squared test and Fisher’s Exact test; to be presented in a future lesson), and summary measure values for OR, RR, and RD and their confidence intervals. The confidence intervals for RR and OR are calculated from the log(RR) and the log(OR) and then back-transformed onto the original scale. COMPARING TWO CATEGORICAL VARIABLES Purpose: To estimate and examine the relationship between two categorical variables using 2x2 tables (counts, proportions, risk difference, relative risk, odds ratio) and graphical representations. For additional resources on RD, RR, or OR, see: Risk Ratios and Rate Ratios (Relative Risk) [Boston University] Measuring Association in Case-Control Studies [Boston University] Understanding Relative Risk, Odds Ratio, and Related Terms [University of Pittsburgh]. For an additional R resource on comparing two categorical variables, see: Two Way Tables [Cyclismo]. Frequencies and Crosstabs [Quick-R]. Bar Plots - R Base Graphs [STHDA]. Stacked Barplot in R [Tutorial Getaway]. Epidemiological 2x2 Contingency Table Analysis Tool [R Documentation]. 10.4 GUIDED QUESTIONS 10.4.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the risk difference (Cohort) of using nicotine gum at the 1st annual visit between females and males? What is its confidence interval? 10.4.0.0.2 Question 2 What is the relative risk of using nicotine gum at the 1st annual visit between females and males? What is its confidence interval? 10.4.0.0.3 Question 3 What is the odds ratio of using nicotine gum at the 1st annual visit between females and males? What is its confidence interval? See 14.17 for answers 10.4.1 Modifying categorical labels If you examine the variable race in a table, do you notice that the labels are not very intuitive? What does race = 1 represent? How about race = 5? It might be more useful to modify the labels of the categorical variable to something meaningful. To change labels of a categorical variable, first, create a new variable in the data set (e.g., lhs$race1), followed by the assignment operator (<-), and then use the factor() function, specifying the variable of interest (e.g., lhs$race), the current categories of the variable (e.g., levels=c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)), and the modified categories of the variable in the same order as in the levels= argument (e.g., labels=c("White","Black","Asian","Native American","Other")): lhs$race1<-factor(lhs$race, levels=c(1,2,3,4,5), labels=c("White","Black","Asian","Native American","Other")) Now when the new variable, race1, is used, the text labels will be displayed rather than the numeric labels found in race. Try it out! mytable2<-table(lhs$race1, lhs$alphagroup) mytable2 barplot(mytable2, main="Barplot of Race by Intervention Group", xlab="Intervention Group", col=c("darkblue","green","yellow","orange","red")) legend(x=2.5,y=1500,legend=rownames(mytable2), fill=c("darkblue","green","yellow","orange","red"), cex=0.5) R TIP You may have noticed that the c() function is used often in R. This function is used when you have multiple values that you have to specify. Also, recall that when you want to create a new variable and add it to an existing data set, you just type ( dataset$variablename<-) followed by what you want that variable to contain (e.g., mapping, modifying, transforming, creating). MODIFYING CATEGORICAL LABELS Purpose: To modify the labels of the categorical variable. For additional R resources on modifying labels of a categorical variable, see: Value Labels [Quick-R]. Renaming levels of a factor [Cookbook for R]. 10.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 10.5.0.0.1 Question 4 How many participants in the LHS study were Asian and in the SI-P intervention group? What proportion of the total is this? 10.5.0.0.2 Question 5 What proportion of the SI-A intervention group were Black? 10.5.0.0.3 Question 6 What proportion of Native Americans were in the UC intervention group? See 14.17 for answers 10.6 CHALLENGE QUESTIONS 10.6.0.0.1 Question 7 Suppose that the LHS researchers were interested in estimating RD, RR, and OR of losing weight by the end of the study (5th visit) between females and males. Create a new variable (wgt5.c) that turns the variable of weight change at the 5th annual visit (wgtchg05) into a categorical variable, with "lost weight" equal to values less than 0, and "gained weight" equal to values greater than or equal to 0. Then, explore the variables of interest by creating a table of sex (AGENDER) and the new variable, weight lost (wgt5.c), and also at least one graphical summary. Then, find estimates for RD, RR, and OR comparing females to males and their confidence intervals. 10.6.0.0.2 Question 8 The Scandinavian simvastatin survival study (4S) was a randomized clinical trial designed to evaluate the effects of the cholesterol-lowering agent simvastatin in patients with coronary heart disease. Over the 5.4 years of follow-up, the treatment group (\(n_1\)=2221) had 111 individuals experience a fatal heart attack. The placebo group (\(n_2\)=2223) had 189 individuals experience such event. Compute RD, RR, and OR and their confidence intervals comparing the two groups on the risk of fatal heart attack. See 14.17.0.1 for answers

Software Lesson 11 Chi-Square Test, Fisher’s Exact Test, and McNemar’s Test in R 11.1 Goal of Software Lesson 11 11.2 Hypothesis test comparing two categorical variables 11.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 11.4 McNemar’s test for paired data 11.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 11.6 CHALLENGE QUESTIONS

Software Lesson 11 Chi-Square Test, Fisher’s Exact Test, and McNemar’s Test in R 11.1 Goal of Software Lesson 11 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to obtain expected counts for a 2x2 table, and obtain inferential results for comparing two categorical variables (e.g., Chi-square test, Fisher’s exact test, and McNemar’s test). Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) 11.2 Hypothesis test comparing two categorical variables When you want to test for a relationship between two categorical variables, you would use Chi-square test, if the expected counts are “large enough”, or Fisher’s exact test, if at least one of the expected counts is small. You are going to revisit the same scenario that was asked in a previous lesson: nicotine gum use at the 1st annual visit (AV1GUM) and gender (AGENDER). The following would be their research question: Is there a relationship between nicotine gum use and gender? 11.2.1 Chi-square test You should use Chi-square test for comparing two categorical variables if the expected counts in each of the cells in the 2x2 table are greater than 5. There are two options for Step 1 when carrying out a Chi-square test in R. The choice of the option depends on the format of your data. You would use Option \(\#1\) if you have the data in a spreadsheet, where each row represents an observation/individual. You would use Option \(\#2\) if you only have the summary values of the data (e.g., counts and totals). Option #1: When have a dataset Step 1: To save a 2x2 table of counts, use the table() function to create a table of the two variables (format: table(ROW, COLUMN)), saving the table under an object name (e.g., mytable<-): ####Step 1a: Create and save the table of the two variables & view it mytable<-table(lhs$AGENDER,lhs$AV1GUM) ####View the table that you just created mytable Option #2: When have only counts and/or totals Suppose instead of having the entire data set, you were only given the count of those who had the event in each group and the total sample size for each group. [Note: This happens a lot in research articles, so it’s good practice to learn how to deal with data that isn’t in a spreadsheet.] Pretend that you were given the following information: Of the 2095 females in the study, 387 of them used nicotine gum at the 1st annual visit. Of the 3493 males in the study, 551 of them used nicotine gum at the 1st annual visit. The code to create this table using the matrix() function is the same code you used in a previous lesson. Step 1: To create a matrix of a 2x2 table of counts, use the matrix() function to create a table of the data, specifying the counts in the c() function in the order of a, b, c, and d (according the table presented in a previous lesson), the number of rows in the table (nrow=2), the number of columns in the table (ncol=2), the order to read in the values (byrow=TRUE fills in the values across the rows), and the row names and column names in the table (dimnames=list(c("Row1Name", "Row2Name"), c("Column1Name", "Column2Name")): ####Step 1b: Create and save the table of the two variables & view it mytable2<-matrix( c(387,2095-387,551,3493-551), #specifying the cell values nrow=2, #specifying the number of rows ncol=2, #specifying the number of columns byrow=TRUE, #create the matrix by rows dimnames=list(c("Female", "Male"), c("Used nicotine gum", "Did not use nicotine gum"))) ####View the table that you just created mytable2 Once you have the table in an object, you can move on to Step 2. Step 2: To carry out Chi-square test, use the chisq.test() function, specifying the name of the object that contains the 2x2 table (e.g., mytable or mytable2) and turning off the Yates’ correction for the Chi-square test (which is used with small sample sizes) (correct=FALSE), and making sure to save the test results under an object name (e.g., mychi.test<-): mychi.test<-chisq.test(mytable, correct=FALSE) ####View the test results mychi.test The default output is the Chi-square test statistic, the degrees of freedom for the test statistic, and the associated p-value. The Chi-square test results were saved under an object name in the above code because there are additional values that are computed in the chisq.test() function and are not presented unless requested. R TIP If you are interested in what values are saved in the chisq.test() function, use the names() function on your saved object that contains the Chi-square test results (e.g., names(mychi.test)). For example, the chisq.test() function calculates the expected counts under the null hypothesis. You may be interested in these values so that you can check the conditions/assumptions of the Chi-square test. To obtain expected counts for the table, use $expected on your saved object for the Chi-square results: mychi.test$expected 11.2.2 Fisher’s exact test Suppose the conditions/assumptions are not met for carrying out a Chi-square test; that is, at least one of the expected counts in the 2x2 table is less than 5. If this occurs, you can use Fisher’s exact test to determine whether the two categorical variables are related or not. To carry out Fisher’s exact test, use the fisher.test() function, specifying the name of the object that contains the 2x2 table (e.g., mytable or mytable2): fisher.test(mytable) The output for the fisher.test() function provides the two-tailed p-value as well as the odds ratio and its confidence interval. HYPOTHESIS TEST COMPARING TWO CATEGORICAL VARIABLES Purpose: To make an inference via hypothesis testing about the relationship between two categorical variables. For additional online websites about Chi-square test and Fisher’s exact test, see: Chi-Square Independence Test: What and Why? The Analysis of Categorical Data: Fisher’s Exact Test For additional R resources on Chi-square test and Fisher’s exact test, see: Chi-square tests for categorical outcomes [BU SPH]. Chi-Square Test of Independence in R [STHDA]. Contingency Tables - Fisher’s Exact Test [R Bloggers]. 11.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 11.3.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the observed count for females who were using nicotine gum at the 1st annual visit? 11.3.0.0.2 Question 2 What is the test statistic and the degrees of freedom from the Chi-square test? What is the resulting p-value from that test? 11.3.0.0.3 Question 3 What is the expected count for females who were using nicotine gum at the 1st annual visit? 11.3.0.0.4 Question 4 What is p-value from Fisher’s exact test? See 14.18 for answers 11.4 McNemar’s test for paired data When the two categorical variables are paired, you would use McNemar’s test. As you have seen, there are situations when you only have summary values of the data rather than the entire data set. You are going to practice again inputting the summary values into a matrix so that you can perform a test for paired data. Researchers investigated the relationship of smoking and death in monozygotic twins in a prospective cohort study. Twenty-two smoking-discordant monozygotic twins were studied to see which twin first succumbed to death. In this study, the smoking-twin died first in 17 of the pairs (matched-pair category c) and the nonsmoking-twin died first in 5 instances (category b). [Note: Zeros can be entered for cells a and d as they don’t contribute to the calculations in the test.] Step 1: To create a matrix of a 2x2 table of counts, use the matrix() function to create a table of the data, specifying the counts in the c() function in the order of a, b, c, and d (according the table presented in a previous lesson), the number of rows in the table (nrow=2), the number of columns in the table (ncol=2), the order to read in the values (byrow=TRUE fills in the values across the rows), and the row names and column names in the table (dimnames=list(c("Row1Name", "Row2Name"), c("Column1Name", "Column2Name")): ####Step 1: Create and save the table of the data & view it mytable.paired<-matrix( c(0,5,17,0), #specifying the cell values nrow=2, #specifying the number of rows ncol=2, #specifying the number of columns byrow=TRUE, #create the matrix by rows dimnames=list(c("Nonsmoker died 1st", "Nonsmoker died 2nd"), c("Smoker died 1st", "Smoker died 2nd"))) ####View the table that you just created mytable.paired Step 2: To carry out McNemar’s test, use the mcnemar.test() function, specifying the name of the object that contains the 2x2 table (e.g., mytable.paired) and turning off the correction for the test (which is used with small sample sizes) (correct=FALSE): mcnemar.test(mytable.paired, correct=FALSE) The code for mcnemar.test() produces a Chi-square test statistic, the degrees of freedom for the test statistic, and the associated p-value. MCNEMAR’S TEST FOR PAIRED DATA Purpose: To make an inference via hypothesis testing about the relationship between two categorical variables that are paired. For additional R resources on McNemar’s test, see: McNemar’s Test [Koji Yatani]. McNemar’s Test for Paired Data [Fasano & Hayden]. 11.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 11.5.0.0.1 Question 5 What is the test statistic and the degrees of freedom from the McNemar’s test? What is the resulting p-value from that test? See 14.18 for answers 11.6 CHALLENGE QUESTIONS 11.6.0.0.1 Question 6 Create a graph to display the relationship between smoking a pipe at baseline (f31pipe) and intervention group (alphagroup). Then, use the the appropriate inferential method for investigating whether this relationship is significant or not. Be sure to check the conditions/assumptions. 11.6.0.0.2 Question 7 Use a McNemar’s test to investigate whether or not there is a relationship between smoking a cigar at baseline (BLCIGAR) and smoking a cigar at the 5th annual visit (A5CIGAR). See 14.18.0.1 for answers

Software Lesson 12 Correlation and Simple Linear Regression in R 12.1 Goal of Software Lesson 12 12.2 Relationship between two numerical variables 12.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 12.4 GUIDED QUESTIONS 12.5 Subsetting a data set 12.6 CHALLENGE QUESTIONS

Software Lesson 12 Correlation and Simple Linear Regression in R 12.1 Goal of Software Lesson 12 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to create a scatterplot, obtain a correlation coefficient, obtain regression coefficients (i.e., intercept and slope) and \(R^2\) for a simple linear regression model, and subset the data. Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) 12.2 Relationship between two numerical variables Thus far, you have examined the relationship between a numerical variable and a categorical variable (e.g., two-sample t-test, ANOVA) and also between two categorical variables (e.g., Chi-square test, Fisher’s exact test, McNemar’s test). Now you are going to investigate the relationship between two numerical variables (e.g., scatterplot, correlation, simple linear regression). In the LHS study, suppose the researchers wanted to examine the relationship between the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline (f10cigs) and FEV1/FVC\(\%\) at baseline (FEVFVC02). This will be the scenario used throughout this section of the lesson. 12.2.1 Scatterplot The first step when examining the relationship between two numerical variables is to make a graphical display called a scatterplot. To make a scatterplot, use the plot() function, specifying the x-variable first and then the y-variable second, separated by a , (e.g., plot(x, y)), and other arguments if you wish to customize the scatterplot (e.g., xlab= to specify the x-axis label, ylab= to specify the y-axis label, main= to specify the plot title): plot(lhs$f10cigs, lhs$FEVFVC02, xlab="Cigarettes per day", ylab="FEV/FVC%", main= "FEV/FVC% vs. Cigarettes at Baseline") SCATTERPLOT Purpose: To graphically display the relationship between two numerical variables. For additional R resources on scatterplots, see: <a href”https://www.statmethods.net/graphs/scatterplot.html“>Scatterplots [Quick-R]. Scatterplots - R Base Graphs [STHDA]. Easy scatterplot using ggplot2 [STHDA] [Note. The {ggplot2} package is a very useful package for creating elegant and aesthetically pleasing visualizations]. 12.2.2 Correlation If the relationship between the two numerical variables looks approximately linear, you can quantify the relationship by computing a correlation coefficient, r. To compute the correlation coefficient, use the cor() function, specifying the x- and y-variables, separated by a ,, and the argument to delete cases that have a mission value in either of the two variables (use="complete.obs"): cor(lhs$f10cigs, lhs$FEVFVC02, use="complete.obs") The use="complete.obs" argument is required in the cor() function if there are missing values in either of the two variables. Without it, R would output NA for the correlation, which you know isn’t true. If there are not missing values in either of those two variables, then you can leave this argument out of the function. CORRELATION Purpose: To quantify the relationship (strength and direction) between two numerical variables. For additional R resources on correlation, see: Correlation Test Between Two Variables in R [STHDA]. Correlation [BU SPH]. 12.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 12.3.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the value of the correlation between the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline and FEV/FVC\(\%\)? See 14.19 for answers 12.3.1 Simple linear regression Besides correlation, another way to describe the relationship between two numerical variables is through simple linear regression (SLR) if the relationship looks approximately linear. In SLR, you obtain regression coefficients (i.e., intercept and slope) from the SLR model to predict Y from X. For example, you can create a SLR model to predict FEV1/FVC\(\%\) at baseline (Y) from the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline (X). To fit a simple linear regression model, first, use the lm() function, specifying the y-variable and the x-variable, separated by ~ (e.g., lm(y~x)) and saving the information under an object name (e.g., model1). Then use the summary() function on the saved lm object (model1) to obtain the regression coefficient values: # fitting a SLR line model1<-lm(lhs$FEVFVC02~lhs$f10cigs) # obtaining results in SLR object, model1 summary(model1) The main information of interest is in the table under the header Coefficients:. The regression coefficient values are under the column Estimate, with the intercept denoted in the first row ((Intercept)) and the slope denoted in the second row. The information in this column is of most interest this week. The standard errors for each of the regression coefficients are under the column Std. Error. This value is used to create confidence intervals and t-test statistics for the coefficients. In the third column, t value represents the t-test statistics for testing the regression coefficients. And lastly, the p-values for testing the regression coefficients are presented in the column Pr(>|t|). These last three pieces of information will be discussed next week. Additional model statistics that may be of interest include \(R^2\), which is denoted next to Multiple R-squared, and \(\hat{\sigma}\), which is denoted next to Residual standard error. \(R^2\) is a measure of how well the model fits the data and the residual standard error, \(\hat{\sigma}\), is the standard deviation of the residual values. 12.3.2 Draw line of best fit The saved lm object can also be used to draw a line of best fit in a scatterplot. To add the line of best fit to a scatterplot, first, use the plot() function, specifying the x- and y-variables and other arguments if you wish to customize the scatterplot, and then use the abline() function on the saved lm object: plot(lhs$f10cigs, lhs$FEVFVC02, xlab="Cigarettes per day", ylab="FEV/FVC%", main= "FEV/FVC% vs. Cigarettes at Baseline") abline(model1) SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION Purpose: To estimate regression coefficients to examine (and predict) the relationship between two numerical variables: a response variable, Y, and an explanatory variable, X. For additional R resources on simple linear regression, see: Simple Linear Regression [R Bloggers]. Doing and Interpreting Simple Linear Regression in R [Berkeley]. 12.4 GUIDED QUESTIONS 12.4.0.0.1 Question 2 What is the value of the intercept in the model of FEVFVC02 vs. f10cigs? What is the value of the slope? 12.4.0.0.2 Question 3 What is the \(R^2\) value for the model? See 14.19 for answers 12.5 Subsetting a data set Sometimes when you are working with a large data set, you are only interested in a small portion of it. For example, suppose the LHS researchers were only interested in the females that were in the study. There are two ways to subset the data in R. The first option is the most basic way by using square brackets after the data name, data[rows, columns]. The second option (and easiest to read) is to use the subset() function. 12.5.1 Option #1 To subset the data set, first, create a new object/data set in R (e.g., lhs.f1), followed by the assignment operator (<-), then assign the new data set to the old one (e.g., lhs), followed by which rows you are interested in keeping (e.g., lhs[lhs$AGENDER=="F", ]): lhs.f1<-lhs[lhs$AGENDER=="F", ] Here’s how you read the line of code: “I want to create a new object/data set, called lhs.f1, (denoted by lhs.f1<-) … and this object is the lhs data set (lhs[ , ]), but only give me the rows for when AGENDER==”F” (denoted by the lhs$AGENDER=="F" before the comma in the square brackets), and give me all of the columns (denoted by the empty space after the comma in the square brackets).” 12.5.2 Option #2 To subset the data set, first, create a new object/data set in R (e.g., lhs.f2), followed by the assignment operator (<-), then use the subset() function, specifying the name of the data set first and then how you want to subset the data (e.g., subset(data, data$variable == value): lhs.f2<-subset(lhs, lhs$AGENDER=="F") Note: For either subsetting option, if you want to subset the data on a range of values for a numerical variable (e.g., age > 50) rather than on a single value (as presented above), then you can just supply that particular criteria in the code. SUBSETTING A DATA SET Purpose: To filter the data set based on specific criteria. For additional R resources on subsetting a data set, see: 5 Ways to Subset a Data Frame in R [R Bloggers]. Subsetting Data [UCLA]. 12.6 CHALLENGE QUESTIONS 12.6.0.0.1 Question 4 Investigate the relationship between the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline (f10cigs; Y) and the number of alcoholic drinks per week at baseline (DRINKSBL; X). Create a scatterplot, calculate a correlation coefficient, and obtain regression coefficients and \(R^2\) from a simple linear regression model. 12.6.0.0.2 Question 5 Investigate the relationship between the FEV1/FVC\(\%\) at baseline (FEVFVC02; Y) and the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline (f10cigs; X) for the data set that contains only females (lhs.f1 or lhs.f2). Create a scatterplot, calculate a correlation coefficient, and obtain regression coefficients and \(R^2\) from a simple linear regression model. 12.6.0.0.3 Question 6 Install and load the {ggplot2} package to create a scatterplot of the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline (f10cigs) and FEV1/FVC\(\%\) at baseline (FEVFVC02). See the following website for tips on how to code using ggplot2: ggplot2 scatterplots: Quick start guide - R software and data visualization. Software Lesson 13 Correlation and Simple Linear Regression in R 13.1 Goal of Software Lesson 13 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to obtain test statistics, -values, and confidence intervals for the regression coefficients, and create diagnostic plots (e.g., residual plot and QQ plot) for the regression model. Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) Last week, you investigated the relationship between two numerical variables via a graphical display (i.e., scatterplots) and summary measures (i.e., correlation, regression coefficients). This week, you are going to build on this knowledge and learn how to obtain inferential results (e.g., CI’s, p-values) and diagnostic information for simple linear regression (SLR). You will be investigating the same scenario as in the previous lesson; that is, the relationship between the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline (f10cigs) and FEV1/FVC\(\%\) at baseline (FEVFVC02). 13.1.1 Inference for Simple Linear Regression Last week, you obtained regression coefficients (i.e., intercept and slope) from the SLR model to predict Y from X. Recall that the code to obtain these values also provided you with inferential results (i.e., p-values) regarding the regression coefficients. This code and explanation are presented again for completeness. 13.1.2 p-values for regression coefficients To fit a simple linear regression model, first, use the lm() function, specifying the y-variable and the x-variable, separated by ~ (e.g., lm(y~x)) and saving the information under an object name (e.g., model1). Then use the summary() function on the saved lm object (model1) to obtain the regression coefficient values: model1<-lm(lhs$FEVFVC02~lhs$f10cigs) summary(model1) As a reminder, the main information of interest is in the table under the header Coefficients:. The regression coefficient values are under the column Estimate, with the intercept denoted in the first row and the slope denoted in the second row. The information in this column was of interest last week. The standard errors for each of the regression coefficients are under the column Std. Error. This value is used to create confidence intervals and t-test statistics for the coefficients. In the third column, t value represents the t-test statistics for testing the regression coefficients. It is found by taking the Estimate divided by Std. Error. And lastly, the p-values for testing the regression coefficients are presented in the column Pr(>|t|). These last three pieces of information are the focus this week. Additional model statistics that may be of interest include \(R^2\), which is denoted next to Multiple R-squared, and \(\hat{\sigma}\), which is denoted next to Residual standard error. \(R^2\) is a measure of how well the model fits the data and the residual standard error, \(\hat{\sigma}\), is the standard deviation of the residual values. 13.1.3 Confidence intervals for regression coefficients Besides testing the regression coefficients against the null hypothesis, you can also produce confidence intervals for the regression coefficients to estimate the population value. To obtain confidence intervals for the regression coefficients, use the confint() function on the saved lm object (model1) and specifying the confidence level (level=): confint(model1, level=0.95) The output displays the lower and upper limits to the \(95\%\) confidence interval for both the intercept and the slope. The default confidence level is \(95\%\), but you can modify the value for the level= argument if you want a different confidence level (e.g., 0.90, 0.99). INFERENCE FOR SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION Purpose: To estimate regression coefficients and their inferential statistics to investigate the relationship between two numerical variables: a response variable, Y, and an explanatory, X. For additional R resources on simple linear regression, see: Simple Linear Regression in R [STHDA]. 13.2 GUIDED QUESTIONS 13.2.0.0.1 Question 1 What is the test statistic for the slope coefficient? What is the resulting p-value from that test? 13.2.0.0.2 Question 2 What is the \(95\%\) confidence interval for the slope coefficient? See 14.20 for answers 13.3 Model diagnostics Another step in SLR is to evaluate how well the model fits the data by creating model diagnostics. From these, you assess the assumptions of the linear regression model (via diagnostic plots, such as a residual plot). 13.3.1 Diagnostic plots To obtain diagnostic plots for a multiple linear regression model, use the autoplot() function from the {ggfortify} package on the saved lm object (model1): ############# # Run the install.packages() function only once per package ############# install.packages("ggfortify") ###Access the package using the library() function library(ggfortify) ####Obtaining diagnostic plots for the model autoplot(model1) As a reminder, four plots will be created in a 2x2 grid. You may need enlarge the Plot window in order to see the four plots clearly. The first plot (top left; first row, first column) is the Residual plot. It is used to assess the linearity and equal variance assumptions. The second plot (top right; first row, second column) is the QQ plot of the residuals from the model and is used to assess the normality assumption of the residuals. The third plot (bottom left; second row, first column) is called the Scale-Location plot and it is a plot of the square root of the standardized residuals vs. the fitted values. This is another plot that can be used to assess the equal variance assumption. The fourth and final plot (bottom right; second row, second column) is the Residuals vs. Leverage plot and is used to help find influential points, if any. MODEL DIAGNOSTICS Purpose: To evaluate the fit and assumptions of the model using plots (e.g., residual plot, QQ plot). For more information on how to evaluate these plots, check out the following website: Understanding Diagnostic Plots for Linear Regression Analysis. For additional R resources on model diagnostics, see: Linear Regression Assumptions and Diagnostics in R: Essentials [STHDA]. Regression Diagnostics [Boston University]. 13.4 CHALLENGE QUESTIONS 13.4.0.0.1 Question 3 Building on a previous challenge question of investigating the relationship between the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline (f10cigs; Y) and the number of alcoholic drinks per week at baseline (DRINKSBL; X), obtain regression coefficients from a simple linear regression model and their inferential statistics (p-values and confidence intervals). Also check the model diagnostics. See 14.20.0.1 for answers

Software Lesson 14 Multiple Linear Regression in R 14.1 Goal of Software Lesson 14 The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to create a a scatterplot with a categorical variable in the plot, obtain regression coefficients, and their test statistics, p-values, and confidence intervals for a multiple linear regression model, and create diagnostic plots for the regression model. Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) data set again. Recall that the data set and a description of data can be found on Canvas, titled lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. lhs<-read.csv(file = file.choose(), header = TRUE) 14.2 Multiple linear regression The previous weeks have been focused on investigating the relationship between two numerical variables (e.g., scatterplot, correlation, simple linear regression). This week builds on that topic, but you will now be investigating the relationship between multiple predictors and a single numerical response variable via multiple linear regression (MLR). In a previous lesson, you examined the relationship between the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline (f10cigs) and FEV1/FVC\(\%\) at baseline (FEVFVC02). Now you are going to add in other predictors to the analysis: gender (AGENDER) and number of alcoholic drinks at baseline (DRINKSBL). 14.2.1 Scatterplot with a categorical variable If you have a model that has a single numerical predictor and a binary numerical predictor, then you can make a graphical display of the response vs. numerical predictor, and denote a categorical predictor within the plot (e.g., change point color/shape/size). This “simple” type of display is presented in this lesson. However, for models with more predictors than the ones just described, we are out of luck for making a graphical display of the relationships because our human brains aren’t capable of thinking in 4D or beyond. Step 1: To create a scatterplot with a categorical variable, use the plot() function, specifying the x-variable first and then the y-variable second, separated by a , (e.g., plot(x, y)), adding the color argument to modify the colors of the points according to the categorical variable, z (e.g., col=c("color1","color2")[factor(data$z)]), and other arguments if you wish to customize the scatterplot (e.g., pch= to change the point shape in the plot, xlab= to specify the x-axis label, ylab= to specify the y-axis label, main= to specify the plot title): plot(x=lhs$f10cigs, y=lhs$FEVFVC02, col=c("darkorange", "mediumturquoise")[factor(lhs$AGENDER)], pch=16, ylab="FEV/FVC at baseline", xlab="Number of cigarettes at baseline (per day)") R TIP You need to specify the same number of colors as there are categories in the categorical variable (e.g., two colors if you have two categories, three colors if you have three categories). However, if you wanted R to set the colors for you, you could have just specified col=factor(lhs$AGENDER) instead of col=c(“darkorange”, “mediumturquoise”)[factor(lhs$AGENDER)]. For more information on names of the colors that are available in R, see the following website: Colors in R [Columbia University]. For more information on shapes of the points ( pch=) that are available in R, see the following website: R plot pch symbols: The different point shapes available in R [STHDA]. When you have this “third” variable in the scatterplot, it’s useful add a legend to denote what the colors in the plot represent. Step 2: To create a legend on the plot, use the legend() function, specifying where you want the legend to be located (e.g., "bottomright", "bottom", "bottomleft", "left", "topleft", "top", "topright", "right", "center"), the text of the legend (e.g., legend=levels(factor(data$z)), or legend=c("Label1", "Label2")), the colors to use for filling the boxes beside the legend text (fill=), and other arguments if you wish to customize the legend (e.g., box.lty= to change the border of the legend box, title= to specify the legend title): legend("bottomright", legend=levels(factor(lhs$AGENDER)), fill=c("darkorange", "mediumturquoise"), box.lty=0) R TIP For more information on adding legends to R plots, see Add legends to plots in R [STHDA]. 14.2.2 Add best-fit lines to scatterplot with a categorical variable If you want to add best-fit lines to the scatterplot with a categorical variable, you have to first fit a model that has the numerical predictor and the categorical predictor in the model, then manually calculate the two best-fit lines for each category, and finally, add the two best-fit lines to the scatterplot. Step 1: To fit a multiple linear regression model, first, use the lm() function, specifying the y-variable and the x-variables (separating the y-variable from the x-variables by ~, separating the x-variables with a + (e.g., lm(y ~ x1 + factor(x2))), and using the factor() function around the categorical x-variable(s)), and saving the information under an object name (e.g., model1). Then use the summary() function on the saved lm object (model1) to obtain the regression coefficient values: model1<-lm(lhs$FEVFVC02 ~ lhs$f10cigs + factor(lhs$AGENDER)) summary(model1) The output is similar to the output for simple linear regression, except you have a row for each predictor in the model. For categorical variables, the row will be labeled with the variable name and the category that R denoted as 1. Based on the output from the summary() function, there is a row denoted factor(lhs$AGENDER)M, which means that row is for the AGENDER categorical variable and the category of interest (1) is M or males and thus the reference category (0) is F or females. Using the results in the output, the equation for this model is: \(\hat{y} = 65.883 - 0.014*x_1 - 0.819*x_2\), where \(y\) = FEV1/FVC\(\%\) at baseline, \(x_1\) = number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline, and \(x_2\) = gender, where 1 = male and 0 = female. Let’s use this information to manually calculate the two best-fit slopes for males and females. For males (\(x_2\) = 1): \(\hat{y}_M = 65.883 - 0.014*x_1 - 0.819*1 = (65.883 - 0.819) - 0.014*x_1 = 65.064 - 0.014*x_1\). So the intercept value for the best-fit line for males is 65.064 and the slope value is -0.014. For females (\(x_2\) = 0): \(\hat{y}_F = 65.883 - 0.014*x_1 - 0.819*0 = 65.883 - 0.014*x_1\). So the intercept value for the best-fit line for females is 65.883 and the slope value is -0.014. Step 2: To add the best-fit lines to the scatterplot with a categorical variable, use the abline() function, twice, specifying the intercept value (a=) and the slope value (b=), and other arguments if you wish to customize the lines (e.g., col= to modify the colors of the lines according to the categorical variable): ### Adding a best-fit line for the males abline(a=65.064, b=-0.014, col="mediumturquoise") ### Adding a best-fit line for the females abline(a=65.883, b=-0.014, col="darkorange") SCATTERPLOT WITH A CATEGORICAL VARIABLE Purpose: To visually examine the relationship between two numerical variables, and denote a categorical variable within the plot. For additional R resources on colors for scatterplots, see: Colors for Plotting [Sleeper, T.]. For additional R resources on useful code for creating (and customizing) various plots, see: ggplot.scatterplot [STHDA]. Graphics with ggplot2 [Quick-R]. qplot: Quick plot with ggplot2 [STHDA]. 14.3 GUIDED QUESTIONS 14.3.0.0.1 Question 1 If colors weren’t specified in the plot() function and the argument was just col=lhs$AGENDER, what are the default colors that R displays? 14.3.0.0.2 Question 2 In the plot() function, what type of symbol is created when pch=16 is specified? 14.3.0.0.3 Question 3 Based on the scatterplot, which gender has the highest number of cigarettes per day at baseline? Which gender has the lowest FEV1/FVC\(\%\) at baseline? See 14.21 for answers 14.4 Multiple linear regression model Let’s examine the effect of the number of cigarettes smoked per day at baseline (f10cigs), gender (AGENDER), and the number of alcoholic drinks at baseline (DRINKSBL) on FEV1/FVC\(\%\) at baseline (FEVFVC02). To fit a multiple linear regression model, first, use the lm() function, specifying the y-variable and the x-variables, (separating the y-variable from the x-variables by ~, separating the x-variables with a + (e.g., lm(y ~ x1 + factor(x2) + x3)), and using the factor() function around the categorical x-variable(s)), and saving the information under an object name (e.g., model2). Then use the summary() function on the saved lm object (model2) to obtain the regression coefficient values: model2<-lm(FEVFVC02 ~ f10cigs + factor(AGENDER) + DRINKSBL, data=lhs) summary(model2) The output is similar to the output shown earlier, except now you have an extra row for the DRINKSBL variable. Additional model statistics that may be of interest include: Adjusted \(R^2\), which is denoted next to Adjusted R-squared. This is a “penalized” measure of how well the MLR model fits the data; and, \(\hat{\sigma}\), which is denoted next to Residual standard error. This is the standard deviation of the residual values. Besides testing the regression coefficients against the null hypothesis, you can also produce confidence intervals for the regression coefficients. To obtain confidence intervals for the regression coefficients, use the confint() function on the saved lm object (model2) and specifying the confidence level (level=): confint(model2, level=0.95) Similar to a previous lesson, the output displays the lower and upper limits to the \(95\%\) confidence interval for the intercept and all of the regression coefficients. The default confidence level is \(95\%\), but you can modify the value for the level= argument if you want a different confidence level (e.g., 0.90, 0.99). MULTIPLE LINEAR REGRESSION MODEL Purpose: To examine the relationship between a numerical response variable and multiple explanatory variables. For additional R resources on multiple linear regression, see: Multiple (Linear) Regression [Quick-R]. Multiple Linear Regression in R [STHDA]. 14.5 GUIDED QUESTIONS 14.5.0.0.1 Question 4 What is the value of the intercept in the model of FEVFVC02 vs. f10cigs, AGENDER, and DRINKSBL? What is the value of the regression coefficient for f10cigs? What is the value of the regression coefficient for AGENDERM? What is the value of the regression coefficient for DRINKSBL? 14.5.0.0.2 Question 5 What is the test statistic for the regression coefficient for f10cigs? What is the resulting p-value from that test? 14.5.0.0.3 Question 6 What is the \(95\%\) confidence interval for the regression coefficient for AGENDERM? See 14.21 for answers 14.6 Diagnostic plots Similar to simple linear regression, you generate diagnostics to evaluate how well the model fits the data. From these, you assess the assumptions of the linear regression model. To obtain diagnostic plots for a multiple linear regression model, use the autoplot() function from the {ggfortify} package on the saved lm object (model2): ############# # Run the install.packages() function only once per package ############# install.packages("ggfortify") ###Access the package using the library() function library(ggfortify) ####Obtaining diagnostic plots for the model autoplot(model2) As a reminder, four plots will be created in a 2x2 grid. You may need enlarge the Plot window in order to see the four plots clearly. The plots are similar to the plots you saw in the simple linear regression lesson, except that the residuals and the fitted values are found by taking into account the multiple predictors in the model. You evaluate the plots in a similar manner as you did in simple linear regression. For more information on how to evaluate the multiple linear regression assumptions, check out the following website: Assumptions of Multiple Linear Regression. DIAGNOSTIC PLOTS Purpose: To evaluate the fit of the regression model to the data using plots (e.g., residual plot, QQ plot). For additional R resources on diagnostic plots, see: Linear Regression Assumptions and Diagnostics in R: Essentials [STHDA]. Regression diagnostic plots [CMU]. 14.7 CHALLENGE QUESTIONS You are going to investigate the effect of the [combined] intervention group (trtgroup; X1) and age started smoking (AGESTART; X2) on the number of cigarettes smoked per day at 2nd annual visit (AV2CIGS; Y). To start, you will need to create a new variable: a categorical variable for the intervention status that has only two categories; one denoting an intervention group (e.g., SI-P or SI-A) and one denoting the control group. 14.7.0.0.1 Question 7 Create a new variable in the data set that takes the randomized intervention group (alphagroup) and recodes the categories into "control" for the usual care (UC) group and "intervention" for the smoking intervention plus Atrovent (SI-A) group and "intervention" for the smoking intervention plus Placebo (SI-P) group. Name this new variable trtgroup. 14.7.0.0.2 Question 8 Create a scatterplot of the three variables (AV2CIGS as Y, AGESTART as X, and trtgroup as the grouping indicator). 14.7.0.0.3 Question 9 Carry out a multiple linear regression for this scenario and obtain regression coefficients and their inferential statistics (p-values and confidence intervals).
